title: Chemistry
description: Chemistry from scratch for complete beginners (in progress): what stuff is made of and how it changes — matter and atoms, the periodic table, bonding and molecules, the mole, reactions, states of matter, gases, solutions, acids and bases, energy and rates, carbon and the chemistry of life. Beginner-first readings, a diagram in nearly every lesson, MCQ practice, tiered easy to hard.

subtopics.title: What Chemistry Is: Matter & the Particle View

lessons.title: What Chemistry Is, and Why It's Everywhere
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Have you ever fried an egg, watched a nail go rusty, or charged a phone? Then you have already met chemistry. In fact, cooking is just chemistry you can eat.

**Build.** **Chemistry** is the study of **matter** — what stuff is made of and how it changes into other stuff. So what is matter? **Matter** is anything that has **mass** (a measure of how much stuff is there) and takes up **space** (it has size, a volume you could in principle measure). That covers an enormous amount: you, the air around you, a glass of water, a rock, a slice of bread. If it is 'stuff,' it is matter, and chemistry is interested in it.

Because matter is everywhere, chemistry is everywhere too. It is the bubbling when you bake, the cleaning power of soap lifting grease off a plate, the way your body digests food and turns it into energy, the reaction inside a battery that lights up a flashlight, and the slow browning of a cut apple. None of this requires a lab coat — it is happening in your kitchen and your body right now.

A chemist's basic move is simple: take some matter, do something to it (heat it, mix it, leave it in the air), and carefully watch what changes. That habit of looking closely is the heart of the subject.

**Recap.** Chemistry is the study of matter — what things are made of and how they change. Matter is anything with mass that takes up space, so chemistry shows up everywhere, from cooking to rust to batteries.

items.prompt: Chemistry is best described as the study of:
choices: Only liquids kept inside a laboratory
Matter — what stuff is made of and how it changes into other stuff
Only living plants and animals
Numbers and equations, with no real-world stuff involved
items.explanation: Chemistry studies matter: what things are made of and how they change. It is not limited to lab liquids (choice 1) or to living things (choice 3), and it is about real physical stuff, not just math on paper (choice 4).

items.prompt: In chemistry, 'matter' means:
choices: Anything that has mass and takes up space
Only things you can pour, like water
Only things heavier than a rock
Anything that gives off light
items.explanation: Matter is anything with mass that takes up space — solids, liquids, and gases all count. It is not limited to pourable liquids (choice 2) or to heavy things (choice 3), and giving off light (choice 4) is not part of the definition.

items.prompt: Which of these is an example of matter?
choices: The air in a balloon
A loud sound
The light from a lamp
The idea of happiness
items.explanation: Air has mass and takes up space (it fills the balloon), so it is matter. A sound (choice 2) and light (choice 3) carry energy but are not stuff with mass and volume, and an idea (choice 4) is not physical at all.

items.prompt: Why is it fair to say 'cooking is chemistry you can eat'?
choices: Because recipes are written by professional chemists
Because cooking takes matter (food) and changes it into new, different stuff
Because food contains no matter until it is cooked
Because only food that is cooked in a laboratory counts as chemistry
items.explanation: Cooking takes matter and changes it — exactly what chemistry studies. Recipes are not written by chemists as a rule (choice 1), raw food is already matter (choice 3), and chemistry is not limited to laboratories (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday event is an example of chemistry at work?
choices: A ball rolling down a hill at steady speed
A book sitting still on a shelf
A shadow growing longer in the evening
A nail slowly turning rusty in the rain
items.explanation: Rust forms when iron in the nail changes into a new substance — a chemical change, which is chemistry. A rolling ball (choice 1) and a longer shadow (choice 3) are about motion and light, and a still book (choice 2) is not changing into new stuff.

items.prompt: 'Mass' is best understood as:
choices: How brightly something glows
The color of an object
How loud something is
A measure of how much stuff something contains
items.explanation: Mass measures how much stuff (matter) is there. Brightness (choice 1), color (choice 2), and loudness (choice 3) are other properties and do not tell you the amount of matter.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'Chemistry only happens in a lab with special equipment.' The best reply is:
choices: True — nothing chemical happens outside a lab
False — chemistry is happening all around us, like digestion, rust, and batteries
True — cooking and cleaning have nothing to do with chemistry
False — but only because labs are too expensive to build
items.explanation: Chemistry is everywhere matter changes — in your body, in rusting metal, in a battery. So the claim is false. Choices 1 and 3 wrongly restrict chemistry to labs, and choice 4 gives an irrelevant reason instead of the real one.

items.prompt: What is a chemist's most basic working habit?
choices: Memorizing long lists without ever looking at anything
Doing something to matter and then carefully observing what changes
Avoiding any kind of change so nothing ever happens
Guessing the answer without testing it
items.explanation: Chemistry is built on doing something to matter (heating, mixing, waiting) and watching closely. Pure memorizing (choice 1) and guessing (choice 4) skip the observation, and avoiding all change (choice 3) means there is nothing to study.

lessons.title: The Particle View: Everything Is Tiny Moving Bits
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Someone opens a bottle of perfume across the room, and a moment later you smell it. Nothing visibly flew toward you — so how did the scent get to your nose?

**Build.** The answer is the single biggest idea in chemistry: the **particle view**. All matter is made of incredibly tiny **particles** — think of them as the smallest bits of stuff, far too small to see even with an ordinary microscope. (Many of these particles are **atoms** or small groups of atoms; for now, just picture tiny bits.) Two facts make this idea powerful. First, these particles are unimaginably small. Second, they are **always moving** — never truly still.

How they move depends on the state of the matter. In a **solid**, the particles are packed tightly and only **jiggle** in place. In a **liquid**, they are looser and **flow** past one another. In a **gas**, they are far apart and **zoom** around quickly. Same kind of particle — just different spacing and speed.

This one picture explains so much. The perfume smell reaches you because scent particles **drift** through the air, mixing with the air particles until some reach your nose. Stir sugar into tea and it seems to 'disappear' — really its particles **spread out** evenly among the water particles, too scattered to see. Heat an ice cube and its particles **speed up** until they break loose and flow as water. You cannot see any of this, yet it quietly explains almost everything ahead.

**Recap.** All matter is made of tiny particles in constant motion: tightly jiggling in solids, flowing in liquids, zooming in gases. This particle view explains smells spreading, sugar dissolving, and ice melting.

items.prompt: The 'particle view' says that all matter is made of:
choices: One smooth, solid, unbroken material with no gaps
Tiny particles that are always moving
Particles that exist only in gases, not in solids
Nothing — matter is empty space
items.explanation: The particle view holds that all matter is built from tiny, constantly moving particles. Matter is not a smooth continuous material (choice 1) — that is the common misconception this idea replaces. Solids have particles too (choice 3), and matter is real stuff, not nothing (choice 4).

items.prompt: You smell perfume from across the room. The particle view explains this by saying:
choices: Smell travels as a beam of light to your eyes
The perfume bottle pushes a solid block of scent across the floor
The perfume particles drift through the air until some reach your nose
Nothing actually moves; your brain invents the smell
items.explanation: Scent particles spread and drift through the air, mixing among the air particles until they reach you. Smell is not light (choice 1), it is not a solid block sliding over (choice 2), and real particles do reach your nose (choice 4).

items.prompt: In a SOLID, the particles mostly:
choices: Are far apart and zoom around quickly
Are packed tightly and only jiggle in place
Have disappeared, leaving empty space
Flow freely and pour like a liquid
items.explanation: Solid particles are packed close and vibrate (jiggle) in fixed spots, which is why solids hold their shape. Far apart and fast describes a gas (choice 1), pouring describes a liquid (choice 4), and the particles do not vanish (choice 3).

items.prompt: When you stir sugar into tea and it seems to 'disappear,' what has really happened to the sugar?
choices: Its particles were destroyed and no longer exist
It turned into water
It floated away into the air as a gas
Its particles spread out evenly among the water particles, too scattered to see
items.explanation: Dissolving spreads the sugar particles evenly among the water particles, so they are still there but too scattered to see (the tea even tastes sweet). The sugar is not destroyed (choice 1), does not become water (choice 2), and does not boil off into the air (choice 3).

items.prompt: How do the particles in a GAS behave compared with those in a solid or liquid?
choices: They are locked in place and cannot move
They are far apart and zoom around quickly
They are packed even tighter than in a solid
They stop moving once the gas fills a room
items.explanation: Gas particles are spread far apart and move fast, which lets a gas spread to fill its container. They are not locked in place (choice 1), they are much farther apart than in a solid (choice 3), and they keep moving constantly (choice 4).

items.prompt: What happens to the particles in an ice cube as it melts into water?
choices: They speed up until they break loose and flow past one another
They slow down and stop moving
They turn into completely different particles
They shrink to half their size
items.explanation: Adding heat makes the particles move faster until they break free of their fixed spots and flow as a liquid. They speed up, not slow down (choice 2); the same particles remain (choice 3); and individual particles do not change size (choice 4).

items.prompt: A beginner pictures water as a smooth, gap-free material rather than as particles. According to the particle view, this picture is:
choices: Correct — matter really is smooth and continuous with no tiny bits
A common misconception — matter is actually made of separate tiny particles
Correct only for water but wrong for everything else
Impossible to decide either way
items.explanation: The whole point of the particle view is that matter is not smooth and continuous — it is made of separate tiny particles. So this is a classic misconception (choice 1 is the error). It applies to all matter, not just water (choice 3), and chemistry has strong evidence for it (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why is the particle view called the big foundational idea of chemistry?
choices: Because it only matters for gases and nothing else
Because this one picture explains so many everyday things, like smells spreading and sugar dissolving
Because it lets you see individual particles with your bare eyes
Because it proves matter has no mass
items.explanation: The particle view is foundational because the same simple picture explains a huge range of observations — melting, smells drifting, dissolving, and more. It applies to all states, not just gases (choice 1); particles are too small to see directly (choice 3); and matter does have mass (choice 4).

lessons.title: Pure Substances vs Mixtures
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Pour yourself a glass of pure water and a glass of salt water. They look the same, but to a chemist they are very different kinds of thing. Why?

**Build.** Chemists sort matter into two groups. A **pure substance** is made of just **one kind of stuff** — every particle in it is the same type. Pure water is one kind of stuff; a bar of pure gold is one kind of stuff. There is nothing else mixed in.

A **mixture** is **two or more kinds of stuff mixed together**. A salad is lettuce, tomato, and dressing all in one bowl. The air you breathe is a mixture of several gases. Salt water is salt mixed into water. In a mixture, each ingredient keeps being itself — the salt is still salt and the water is still water, just blended.

Here is a handy clue: because the parts of a mixture keep their own identity, a mixture can often be **separated** back into its ingredients. Let salt water sit in the sun and the water drifts off as a gas, leaving the salt behind. You could pick the tomatoes out of a salad. A pure substance, made of only one kind of stuff, cannot be split this way — there is nothing else in there to separate.

A common trap is to call salt water 'pure' because it looks clean and clear. Looks do not decide it: salt water contains two different kinds of stuff, so it is a mixture, not a pure substance.

**Recap.** A pure substance is one kind of stuff (pure water, pure gold); a mixture is two or more kinds mixed together (salad, air, salt water) and can often be separated again. Looking clear does not make something pure.

items.prompt: A 'pure substance' is matter that is made of:
choices: Two or more kinds of stuff blended together
Just one kind of stuff
Only liquids, never solids
Anything as long as it looks clean
items.explanation: A pure substance contains only one kind of stuff, with every particle the same type. Two or more kinds blended is a mixture (choice 1). Pure substances can be solid, liquid, or gas (choice 3), and looking clean does not decide purity (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which of these is a MIXTURE?
choices: A bar of pure gold
A sample of pure water
A bowl of salad
A single kind of particle by itself
items.explanation: A salad is several different foods combined, so it is a mixture. Pure gold (choice 1) and pure water (choice 2) are each one kind of stuff, and a single kind of particle (choice 4) is the opposite of a mixture.

items.prompt: Is the air you breathe a pure substance or a mixture?
choices: A pure substance, because it is invisible
A mixture, because it is several different gases together
A pure substance, because it has no mass
Neither, because gases are not matter
items.explanation: Air is several gases (such as nitrogen and oxygen) mixed together, so it is a mixture. Being invisible (choice 1) does not make it pure, air does have mass (choice 3), and gases are matter (choice 4).

items.prompt: Someone argues that salt water is a pure substance because it looks perfectly clear. The best response is:
choices: Agree — if it looks clear, it must be pure
Disagree — salt water contains two kinds of stuff (salt and water), so it is a mixture
Agree — water always makes things pure
Disagree — but only because salt water is cloudy
items.explanation: Looking clear does not make something pure; salt water has two kinds of stuff and is a mixture. Choices 1 and 3 fall for the 'clear means pure' trap, and choice 4 gives a wrong reason since salt water is actually clear.

items.prompt: Why can a mixture often be separated back into its ingredients?
choices: Because each ingredient keeps being itself within the mixture
Because mixtures contain only one kind of stuff
Because separating always destroys the ingredients
Because mixtures are not really matter
items.explanation: In a mixture, the parts keep their own identity (salt stays salt, water stays water), so they can be pulled apart again. A mixture has more than one kind of stuff (choice 2), separating recovers the parts rather than destroying them (choice 3), and mixtures are matter (choice 4).

items.prompt: If you leave salt water sitting in the sun until the water drifts away as a gas, what is left behind?
choices: Nothing at all
The salt
Pure gold
A brand-new substance that was never there before
items.explanation: The water leaves as a gas and the salt stays behind, showing salt water was a mixture you can separate. Something remains, so not nothing (choice 1); it is salt, not gold (choice 3); and no new substance is created — the salt was there all along (choice 4).

items.prompt: In the particle picture, how does a pure substance look different from a mixture?
choices: A pure substance has all-same particles; a mixture has different kinds mixed together
A pure substance has different kinds of particles; a mixture has all-same particles
Both look exactly the same at the particle level
Neither one is made of particles
items.explanation: Picture it as circles: a pure substance is all identical circles, while a mixture has different shapes mixed in. Choice 2 reverses this, choice 3 ignores the difference, and choice 4 forgets that all matter is made of particles.

items.prompt: Which list contains ONLY pure substances?
choices: Salad, air, salt water
Pure gold, a bowl of cereal with milk
Air, lemonade
Pure water, pure gold
items.explanation: Pure water and pure gold are each a single kind of stuff. Salad, air, salt water, cereal-with-milk, and lemonade are all mixtures of several things, so choices 1, 2, and 3 each include at least one mixture.

lessons.title: Physical vs Chemical Changes
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Melt a stick of butter in a warm pan and it turns to liquid. Crack an egg into that same pan and it sets into a firm white. Both 'changed' — but only one became something genuinely new. Spotting the difference is a key chemistry skill.

**Build.** Chemists split changes into two kinds. A **physical change** does **not** make a new substance — the stuff is the same, just in a different form. Melting ice into water is physical: it is still water, just liquid now. Dissolving sugar in tea is physical: the sugar particles only spread out. Cutting a sheet of paper is physical: smaller pieces, but still paper. A useful sign of a physical change is that you can often **reverse** it — freeze the water back into ice, and you are right where you started.

A **chemical change** is different: it makes a **brand-new substance** with new properties. Burning wood turns it into ash and smoke — you cannot un-burn it back into a log. Iron rusting turns shiny metal into crumbly orange rust. Baking a cake turns runny batter into something you could never separate back into flour and eggs. A battery works by chemical change. Chemical changes are usually **hard to reverse**, because the original stuff is genuinely gone.

The big question to ask is simply: **did I end up with new stuff, or the same stuff in a new form?** New stuff means a chemical change. Same stuff, new form, means a physical change. A common trap is thinking that dissolving sugar is chemical — it is not, because no new substance forms; the sugar is still sugar.

**Recap.** A physical change keeps the same substance in a new form (melting, dissolving, cutting) and is often reversible. A chemical change makes a new substance (burning, rusting, baking) and is usually hard to reverse. Ask: new stuff, or same stuff?

items.prompt: The key difference between a physical change and a chemical change is:
choices: A physical change makes a new substance; a chemical change does not
Both always make brand-new substances
A chemical change makes a new substance; a physical change does not
Neither one ever changes matter in any way
items.explanation: A chemical change produces new stuff with new properties, while a physical change leaves the same substance in a new form. Choice 1 reverses the definitions — a common mistake. Physical changes do not make new substances (choice 2), and both clearly change matter (choice 4).

items.prompt: Melting an ice cube into water is which kind of change?
choices: A chemical change, because the ice is destroyed
A physical change, because it is still water, just in a new form
A chemical change, because a new substance forms
Neither, because nothing happens at all
items.explanation: Melting is physical: solid water becomes liquid water, the same substance in a different form, and you can freeze it back. No new substance forms (choices 1 and 3 are the misconception), and a real change does occur (choice 4).

items.prompt: Burning a sheet of paper into ash and smoke is which kind of change?
choices: A physical change, because the paper is just in a new form
A chemical change, because new substances (ash and smoke) are formed
A physical change, because you can easily turn the ash back into paper
Neither, because burning is not a change
items.explanation: Burning makes brand-new substances — ash and smoke — so it is a chemical change, and you cannot un-burn it. It is not merely a new form of paper (choice 1), the ash cannot be turned back into paper (choice 3), and burning is certainly a change (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says dissolving sugar in water is a chemical change. Why is that wrong?
choices: Because no new substance forms — the sugar is still sugar, just spread out
Because dissolving destroys the sugar forever
Because dissolving turns the sugar into salt
Because dissolving only works with chemical changes
items.explanation: Dissolving is physical: the sugar particles simply spread among the water, and the sugar is still sugar (the water tastes sweet). No new substance appears, so calling it chemical is the classic error. The sugar is not destroyed (choice 2) and does not become salt (choice 3); choice 4 misstates what dissolving is.

items.prompt: Frying an egg until it sets is a chemical change because:
choices: The egg is simply changing shape but stays the same stuff
The runny egg turns into a new, firm substance you cannot turn back
Frying never changes any food
The egg is only melting like butter
items.explanation: Cooking the egg creates a new substance — the firm cooked white — that cannot be returned to a raw egg, so it is chemical. It is not just a shape change (choice 1), frying does change food (choice 3), and the egg is not melting (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which group lists only PHYSICAL changes?
choices: Burning wood, iron rusting, baking a cake
Frying an egg, rusting a nail, burning toast
Baking bread, a battery working, rust forming
Melting ice, dissolving sugar, cutting paper
items.explanation: Melting, dissolving, and cutting all keep the same substance in a new form, so all are physical. The other groups contain burning, rusting, and baking, which make new substances and are therefore chemical changes.

items.prompt: Which single question best tells a physical change from a chemical change?
choices: Did the change happen quickly or slowly?
Did I end up with new stuff, or the same stuff in a new form?
Did the change happen in a kitchen or a lab?
Was the object large or small to begin with?
items.explanation: The deciding question is whether a new substance formed (chemical) or the same substance just took a new form (physical). Speed (choice 1), location (choice 3), and size (choice 4) do not determine which kind of change it is.

items.prompt: Ice melting to water and freezing back to ice can be repeated over and over. Burning toast cannot be undone. This shows that:
choices: Physical changes are often reversible, while chemical changes are usually hard to reverse
Chemical changes are always easy to reverse
Physical changes can never be reversed
Reversibility has nothing to do with the type of change
items.explanation: Because a physical change keeps the same substance, it can often be reversed; a chemical change makes new stuff and is usually hard to reverse. Chemical changes are not easy to reverse (choice 2), physical changes often can be reversed (choice 3), and reversibility is a real clue (choice 4).

lessons.title: Signs of a Chemical Change, and the Observer's Mindset
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You mix two clear liquids and suddenly the cup fizzes, warms up, and turns cloudy. Something new just formed — but how would you know for sure? Chemists watch for a few telltale signs.

**Build.** A **chemical change** makes a new substance, and it usually announces itself with one or more clues. The main signs to watch for are: a **color change** (an apple slice browning, a leaf turning red); a **gas given off**, often seen as **bubbles** (vinegar and baking soda fizzing); **heat or light released** (a flame, hand-warmers getting hot); and a **solid forming** out of liquids, which chemists call a **precipitate** (a cloudy solid appearing when two clear liquids meet). On top of these, chemical changes are usually **hard to reverse** — you cannot easily get the original stuff back.

Notice that a physical change shows **none** of these new-substance signs. Melting ice or dissolving sugar produces no new color, no gas, no released heat, and no precipitate — just the same stuff in a new form. So the signs are a practical checklist: spot one, and a chemical change has likely happened.

All of this rests on one habit: **careful observation**. Chemistry is learned by paying close attention — noting what you see, smell, and feel changing, ideally writing it down. And one plain rule of the mindset: treat every chemical with respect and follow safe handling, because some substances can harm you even when a change looks calm.

**Recap.** Signs of a chemical change are a color change, a gas or bubbles, heat or light given off, and a solid (precipitate) forming — and it is usually hard to reverse. Physical changes show none of these. Above all, observe carefully and handle chemicals safely.

items.prompt: Which of these is a sign that a CHEMICAL change has happened?
choices: The ingredients keep exactly the same color, smell, and form
Bubbles of a gas are given off
An ice cube simply gets smaller as it melts
Sugar quietly spreads through tea with no other change
items.explanation: Producing a gas (bubbles) is a classic sign of a chemical change. Staying exactly the same (choice 1) signals no chemical change, and melting ice (choice 3) and dissolving sugar (choice 4) are physical changes with none of the telltale signs.

items.prompt: Which list gives common signs of a chemical change?
choices: Getting colder only, with no other change
Changing shape, then easily changing back
Color change, gas or bubbles, heat or light, a solid forming
Looking clear, looking clean, looking shiny
items.explanation: The standard signs are a color change, gas/bubbles, heat or light given off, and a solid (precipitate) forming. Choice 2 describes an easily reversed physical change, and choices 1 and 4 are not the recognized signs of new-substance formation.

items.prompt: In chemistry, a 'precipitate' is:
choices: A gas that escapes into the air
A solid that forms, often when two liquids are mixed
The heat given off during a reaction
A color that fades away to nothing
items.explanation: A precipitate is a solid that appears — for example, a cloudy solid forming when two clear liquids are combined — and it signals a chemical change. It is not a gas (choice 1), not heat (choice 3), and not a fading color (choice 4).

items.prompt: You melt some ice and watch closely. Which is true about the signs of a chemical change?
choices: You see all four signs, so melting is chemical
You see none of the signs, which fits melting being a physical change
You see bubbles of a new gas, so melting is chemical
You see a precipitate form, so melting is chemical
items.explanation: Melting ice produces no new color, no gas, no released heat, and no precipitate — it shows none of the signs because it is a physical change. Claiming you see those signs (choices 1, 3, 4) misreads what happens when ice simply turns to water.

items.prompt: Besides the visible signs, what else is usually true of a chemical change?
choices: It can always be reversed in a single easy step
It never gives off heat or light
It leaves the substance completely unchanged
It is usually hard to reverse, because the original stuff is genuinely gone
items.explanation: Because a chemical change makes new substances, the original is gone and it is usually hard to undo. It is not easily reversible (choice 1), it often does give off heat or light (choice 2), and the substance is changed, not left the same (choice 3).

items.prompt: Vinegar poured on baking soda fizzes vigorously. The fizzing is a sign of a chemical change because it shows:
choices: A gas is being produced
The mixture is simply getting colder with no new stuff
The baking soda is only changing shape
Nothing new is happening
items.explanation: The fizz is a gas being given off — one of the clearest signs that a new substance formed. It is not a mere temperature drop with no new stuff (choice 2) or a shape change (choice 3), and something new is clearly happening (choice 4).

items.prompt: How is chemistry mainly learned, according to the observer's mindset?
choices: By never looking at anything and only guessing
By careful observation — noting what you see, smell, and feel changing
By assuming nothing ever changes
By memorizing colors without watching any change
items.explanation: Chemistry is learned by observing closely and noting changes you see, smell, and feel. Guessing without looking (choice 1), assuming nothing changes (choice 3), and rote memorizing with no observation (choice 4) all skip the careful watching at the heart of the subject.

items.prompt: What is the sensible safety attitude when working with chemicals?
choices: Treat every chemical with respect and follow safe handling
Assume anything that looks calm is completely harmless
Taste unknown substances to identify them
Ignore safety because chemistry is always gentle
items.explanation: Respect chemicals and handle them safely, since some can harm you even when a change looks calm. Assuming calm-looking substances are harmless (choice 2), tasting unknowns (choice 3), and ignoring safety (choice 4) are all unsafe.

lessons.title: Meet the Atom and Its Three Parts
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You already know that all matter is made of tiny particles. Now we zoom in on the most important particle of all: the **atom**. Atoms are the building blocks that everything is made of, like letters that build every word, or LEGO bricks that build everything in a toy box.

**Build.** An **atom** is the smallest piece of an **element** that is still that element. (An **element** is just one basic kind of stuff, like gold, oxygen, or carbon.) Atoms are unimaginably small — far too tiny to see. Long ago people thought the atom was a solid ball that could not be split, which is even what its name first meant. Today we know better: an atom is actually made of three smaller parts.

Two of those parts sit packed together in a tiny, dense center called the **nucleus**. The first is the **proton**, which carries a **positive charge**, written **+**. The second is the **neutron**, which carries **no charge**, written **0**. The third part is the **electron**, which carries a **negative charge**, written **−**, and it lives in the space *around* the nucleus, not inside it.

Why do the electrons stay near? Because **opposite charges attract**: the positive (+) nucleus pulls the negative (−) electrons and holds them close. Two more friendly facts: an atom is mostly **empty space** — the nucleus is tiny compared with the whole atom — and electrons are far **lighter** than protons and neutrons.

**Recap.** An atom is the smallest piece of an element. It has protons (+) and neutrons (0) in a tiny central nucleus, with light electrons (−) in the mostly empty space around it. Opposite charges attract, so the + nucleus holds the − electrons.

items.prompt: An atom is best described as:
choices: A kind of liquid found only in laboratories
The smallest piece of an element that is still that element
A large object you can easily see with your eyes
Another word for a mixture of many substances
items.explanation: An atom is the smallest piece of an element that is still that element — the basic building block of matter. It is not a lab liquid (choice 1), it is far too small to see (choice 3), and it is a single building block, not a mixture (choice 4).

items.prompt: What are the three parts of an atom, and their charges?
choices: Protons (negative), neutrons (positive), electrons (no charge)
Protons (no charge), neutrons (negative), electrons (positive)
Protons (positive +), neutrons (no charge 0), electrons (negative −)
Protons, neutrons, and electrons all carry a positive charge
items.explanation: Protons are positive (+), neutrons have no charge (0), and electrons are negative (−). Choice 1 and choice 2 scramble the charges, and choice 4 is wrong because the three parts do not all share the same charge — if they did, nothing would attract.

items.prompt: The tiny, dense center of an atom is called the:
choices: Nucleus
Electron
Element
Mixture
items.explanation: The nucleus is the small, dense center that holds the protons and neutrons. An electron (choice 2) is a part that moves around the nucleus, an element (choice 3) is a kind of stuff, and a mixture (choice 4) is several substances together.

items.prompt: Where are the electrons found in an atom?
choices: Packed tightly inside the nucleus next to the protons
In the space around the nucleus
Floating completely outside the atom, with no connection to it
Nowhere — atoms do not have electrons
items.explanation: Electrons live in the space around the nucleus, not inside it. Thinking electrons sit in the nucleus (choice 1) is a common mistake — only protons and neutrons are in the nucleus. They are part of the atom, not unconnected (choice 3), and every atom has electrons (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which two parts of an atom are found together in the nucleus?
choices: Protons and electrons
Electrons and neutrons
Protons and neutrons
Electrons only
items.explanation: Protons and neutrons are packed together in the nucleus. Electrons are not in the nucleus — they are in the space around it — so choices 1, 2, and 4 all wrongly place electrons inside the nucleus.

items.prompt: Why do the electrons stay near the nucleus instead of drifting away?
choices: Opposite charges attract: the positive nucleus pulls the negative electrons
Like charges attract: both are negative, so they stick
The neutrons glue the electrons in place with their charge
There is no reason — electrons actually fly away at once
items.explanation: Opposite charges attract, so the positive (+) nucleus pulls and holds the negative (−) electrons. Like charges actually push apart, not attract (choice 2); neutrons have no charge, so they cannot pull electrons (choice 3); and the electrons do stay, held by the pull (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which statement about the size and make-up of an atom is correct?
choices: An atom is a solid, packed ball with no empty space inside
An atom is mostly empty space, with a tiny nucleus at its center
An atom is mostly nucleus, with almost no empty space
An atom has no center at all
items.explanation: An atom is mostly empty space, with the protons and neutrons crowded into a tiny central nucleus. It is not a solid packed ball (choice 1) — that was the old idea — the nucleus is tiny, not most of the atom (choice 3), and the atom does have a center, the nucleus (choice 4).

items.prompt: Compared with protons and neutrons, electrons are:
choices: Much heavier
Exactly the same weight
Too heavy to move at all
Much lighter
items.explanation: Electrons are much lighter than protons and neutrons, which is why almost all of an atom's mass is in the nucleus. They are not heavier (choice 1) or the same weight (choice 2), and being light, they move easily rather than being stuck (choice 4).

items.prompt: The comparison 'atoms are like letters that build every word' is meant to show that:
choices: Atoms can be read out loud like an alphabet
A few kinds of building block can combine to build a huge variety of things
Atoms are exactly the size of printed letters
Only words, not real objects, are made of atoms
items.explanation: Just as a small set of letters builds countless words, atoms are building blocks that combine to make all the matter around us. The analogy is not about reading atoms (choice 1) or their printed size (choice 3), and real objects — not just words — are made of atoms (choice 4).

lessons.title: Atomic Number: The Atom's ID
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Here is a puzzle. Every atom is built from the same three parts — protons, neutrons, electrons. So what makes a gold atom different from an oxygen atom? The answer is surprisingly simple, and it comes down to one number.

**Build.** The **atomic number** of an atom is just the **number of protons** in its nucleus. And that number decides **which element** the atom is. Think of the proton count as the atom's **ID number**, like a number that says exactly who it is.

The rule is strict: a certain number of protons always means the same element. An atom with **1 proton** is **hydrogen** — always. An atom with **2 protons** is **helium**. An atom with **6 protons** is **carbon**. An atom with **8 protons** is **oxygen**. You never have to guess: count the protons, and you know the element.

This also means the proton count is the atom's true identity. If you could somehow **change the number of protons**, you would no longer have the same element — it would turn into a different element entirely. (Adding or removing a neutron or an electron does *not* do this, as we will see; only the proton count sets the element.) So protons are special: they are the one count that says what kind of atom you are holding.

**Recap.** The atomic number is the number of protons, and it tells you which element the atom is. 1 proton is hydrogen, 2 is helium, 6 is carbon, 8 is oxygen. Change the proton count and you change the element itself.

items.prompt: The 'atomic number' of an atom is the number of:
choices: Protons in its nucleus
Neutrons in its nucleus
Electrons plus neutrons added together
Particles you can see with your eyes
items.explanation: The atomic number is defined as the number of protons. It is not the neutron count (choice 2) — a common mix-up — nor electrons plus neutrons (choice 3), and atomic parts are far too small to see (choice 4).

items.prompt: What does the number of protons tell you about an atom?
choices: How fast the atom is moving
Which element the atom is
What color the atom is
Nothing useful at all
items.explanation: The proton count is the atom's identity — it tells you which element it is. It does not set the atom's speed (choice 1) or color (choice 3), and it is in fact the single most important number for naming the atom (choice 4).

items.prompt: An atom has exactly 6 protons. Which element is it?
choices: Hydrogen
Helium
It is impossible to tell from the protons
Carbon
items.explanation: 6 protons always means carbon — the proton count alone tells you the element. Hydrogen has 1 proton (choice 1) and helium has 2 (choice 2), and because protons set the identity, it is never impossible to tell (choice 3).

items.prompt: Which atom is hydrogen?
choices: The atom with 1 proton
The atom with 6 protons
The atom with 8 protons
Any atom that has neutrons
items.explanation: Hydrogen is the atom with 1 proton. 6 protons is carbon (choice 2) and 8 protons is oxygen (choice 3). Having neutrons does not make an atom hydrogen (choice 4) — only the proton count decides the element.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'The number of neutrons is what decides which element an atom is.' Why is this wrong?
choices: It is actually correct — neutrons set the element
Because the number of protons, not neutrons, decides the element
Because elements do not depend on any particle count
Because only electrons decide the element
items.explanation: The element is set by the number of protons, not neutrons — thinking neutrons decide the element is a classic error. The claim is therefore not correct (choice 1); elements do depend on a particle count, the protons (choice 3); and electrons do not set the element either (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why is the proton count compared to an 'ID number'?
choices: Because it is printed on the outside of every atom
Because it identifies exactly which element the atom is
Because it changes randomly from moment to moment
Because every element shares the very same proton count
items.explanation: Like an ID number, the proton count picks out exactly which element the atom is. It is not literally printed on atoms (choice 1), it does not change on its own (choice 3), and each element has its own distinct proton count rather than sharing one (choice 4).

items.prompt: Suppose you could change an atom's number of protons from 6 to 8. What happens?
choices: Nothing — it stays the same element
It becomes a different element (it changes from carbon to oxygen)
It stops being matter
Only its color changes, but it is still carbon
items.explanation: Changing the proton count changes the element: 6 protons is carbon and 8 protons is oxygen, so it becomes a different element. It does not stay the same (choice 1), it is still matter (choice 3), and it is no longer carbon at all (choice 4).

items.prompt: An atom has 2 protons. What is its atomic number, and which element is it?
choices: Atomic number 2, helium
Atomic number 6, carbon
Atomic number 0, no element
Atomic number 2, but the element cannot be known
items.explanation: The atomic number equals the proton count, so 2 protons means atomic number 2, which is helium. Carbon has 6 protons (choice 2), an atom with protons does not have atomic number 0 (choice 3), and the proton count does tell us the element (choice 4).

lessons.title: Mass Number and Counting the Parts
lessons.content:
**Hook.** We can already name an atom by counting its protons. But how 'heavy' is an atom? For that we need one more simple count — and it is just addition you can do on your fingers.

**Build.** The **mass number** of an atom is the **number of protons plus the number of neutrons**. We add these two because protons and neutrons are the heavy parts, sitting in the nucleus. Electrons are so light that they add almost nothing, so we **leave electrons out** of the mass number.

Let us count a carbon atom. Ordinary carbon has **6 protons** and **6 neutrons**. So its mass number is 6 + 6 = **12**. Try another: an atom with **8 protons** and **8 neutrons** has a mass number of 8 + 8 = **16**.

There is one more balance to know. In a **neutral atom** — an atom with no overall charge — the number of **electrons equals the number of protons**. The positive (+) protons and the negative (−) electrons are equal in number, so their charges **cancel out** to zero. For neutral carbon: 6 protons and 6 electrons, which balance. So for that carbon atom we can count all three: **6 protons, 6 neutrons, and 6 electrons.**

**Recap.** Mass number = protons + neutrons (electrons are too light to count). Neutral carbon has 6 protons + 6 neutrons = mass number 12, and it has 6 electrons to balance its 6 protons. In any neutral atom, protons and electrons are equal in number.

items.prompt: The 'mass number' of an atom is:
choices: The number of protons only
The number of protons plus the number of neutrons
The number of electrons plus the number of protons
The number of neutrons minus the number of protons
items.explanation: Mass number is protons plus neutrons — the two heavy parts in the nucleus. Protons alone is the atomic number, not the mass number (choice 1); electrons are too light to include (choice 3); and you add, not subtract (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why are electrons left out of the mass number?
choices: Because electrons are much lighter than protons and neutrons
Because electrons are much heavier than protons
Because atoms have no electrons
Because electrons are the same weight as neutrons
items.explanation: Electrons are so light that they add almost nothing to the mass, so only protons and neutrons are counted. They are not heavier than protons (choice 2), atoms do have electrons (choice 3), and they are far lighter than neutrons, not equal (choice 4).

items.prompt: An ordinary carbon atom has 6 protons and 6 neutrons. What is its mass number?
items.explanation: Mass number = protons + neutrons = 6 + 6 = 12. Choice 1 counts only the protons, choice 3 multiplies instead of adding, and choice 0 ignores the parts entirely.

items.prompt: In a neutral atom (no overall charge), how do the numbers of protons and electrons compare?
choices: There are always more protons than electrons
The number of electrons equals the number of protons
There are always more electrons than protons
The number of electrons equals the number of neutrons
items.explanation: A neutral atom has equal protons and electrons, so the + and − charges cancel to zero. It is not lopsided toward protons (choice 1) or electrons (choice 3), and the balance is with protons, not neutrons (choice 4).

items.prompt: A neutral carbon atom has 6 protons and 6 neutrons. How many electrons does it have?
choices: 0 electrons
12 electrons
3 electrons
6 electrons
items.explanation: In a neutral atom the electrons equal the protons, so 6 protons means 6 electrons. It is not zero (choice 1); 12 is the mass number, not the electron count (choice 2); and 3 does not balance the 6 protons (choice 3).

items.prompt: A neutral atom has 8 protons and 8 neutrons. Count its parts.
choices: 8 protons, 8 neutrons, 8 electrons
8 protons, 8 neutrons, 0 electrons
8 protons, 16 neutrons, 8 electrons
16 protons, 8 neutrons, 8 electrons
items.explanation: Protons are given as 8 and neutrons as 8. Because the atom is neutral, electrons equal protons, so there are 8 electrons too. A neutral atom is not missing its electrons (choice 2), and choices 3 and 4 change the given proton or neutron counts.

items.prompt: An atom has 8 protons and 8 neutrons. What is its mass number?
items.explanation: Mass number = protons + neutrons = 8 + 8 = 16. Choice 1 counts protons only, choice 3 multiplies instead of adding, and choice 0 ignores the parts.

items.prompt: Why do the charges in a neutral atom add up to zero?
choices: Because neutrons cancel out the protons
Because the equal numbers of positive protons and negative electrons cancel each other
Because atoms have no charged parts at all
Because the electrons are heavier than the protons
items.explanation: Equal numbers of + protons and − electrons cancel, giving an overall charge of zero. Neutrons have no charge, so they do not do the canceling (choice 1); atoms do have charged parts (choice 3); and electron mass is irrelevant to charge balance (choice 4).

lessons.title: Isotopes and Ions
lessons.content:
**Hook.** We said the proton count never changes the element. But the *other* two parts — neutrons and electrons — can vary. Changing them gives us two new ideas with friendly names: **isotopes** and **ions**.

**Build.** First, **isotopes**. Isotopes are atoms of the **same element** (so the **same number of protons**) but with a **different number of neutrons**. Same protons means same identity and the **same chemistry**; different neutrons means a slightly **different mass**. Picture the same model of car carrying different amounts of cargo — same car, slightly different weight. For example, **carbon-12** has 6 protons and 6 neutrons, while **carbon-14** has 6 protons and 8 neutrons. Both are still carbon, because both have 6 protons; only the neutron count (and so the mass) differs. A common trap is to think isotopes are *different elements* — they are not, because the protons are the same.

Next, **ions**. An **ion** is an atom that has **gained or lost electrons**, so it is no longer neutral and now carries a charge. Gaining extra electrons adds negative charge, making a **negative ion**. Losing electrons leaves extra positive charge behind, making a **positive ion**. The key rule: for an ion, the **protons never change** — only the electrons do. (If the protons changed, it would be a different element, not an ion.) This idea of charged atoms will matter later when atoms stick together.

**Recap.** Isotopes are the same element with different neutron counts (carbon-12 vs carbon-14) — same chemistry, different mass. Ions are atoms that gained electrons (negative) or lost electrons (positive); the proton count stays the same. Neutrons change mass; electrons change charge; protons set the element.

items.prompt: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have:
choices: A different number of protons
A different number of neutrons
No nucleus
A different number of nuclei
items.explanation: Isotopes share the same element (same protons) but differ in their number of neutrons. A different number of protons would make a different element, not an isotope (choice 1); isotopes still have a nucleus (choice 3); and each atom still has just one nucleus (choice 4).

items.prompt: Carbon-12 has 6 protons and 6 neutrons; carbon-14 has 6 protons and 8 neutrons. These two are:
choices: Two completely different elements
Isotopes of carbon — the same element with different neutron counts
The same atom with a different number of protons
Not real, because carbon can have only one form
items.explanation: Both have 6 protons, so both are carbon; only their neutron counts differ, which makes them isotopes. They are not different elements (choice 1) — that is the classic isotope misconception — their proton counts are the same, not different (choice 3), and carbon really does have more than one isotope (choice 4).

items.prompt: Two isotopes of the same element differ in mass but behave the same in chemistry. Why is their chemistry the same?
choices: Because they have the same number of protons (same element)
Because they have the same number of neutrons
Because they have no electrons
Because mass is the only thing that affects chemistry
items.explanation: Same protons means the same element, and that is what gives the same chemistry. Isotopes differ in neutrons, so that is not what they share (choice 2); they do have electrons (choice 3); and it is the proton-based identity, not the small mass difference, that controls their chemistry (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'Carbon-12 and carbon-14 must be different elements because their numbers are different.' The best correction is:
choices: They are different elements, since any difference means a new element
They are the same element (carbon) because both have 6 protons; only the neutrons differ
They are the same element only because they have different neutrons
They cannot be compared at all
items.explanation: Both have 6 protons, so both are carbon — the element is set by protons, and only the neutron count differs. Not every difference makes a new element (choice 1); it is the same protons, not the different neutrons, that makes them the same element (choice 3); and they certainly can be compared (choice 4).

items.prompt: An ion is:
choices: An atom that has gained or lost electrons, so it carries a charge
An atom that has gained or lost protons
An atom with no nucleus
Another word for an isotope
items.explanation: An ion is an atom that has gained or lost electrons, leaving it with an overall charge. It is not about gaining or losing protons (choice 2) — changing protons would change the element — ions still have a nucleus (choice 3), and an ion is not the same as an isotope (choice 4).

items.prompt: An atom GAINS one or more electrons. What kind of ion does it become?
choices: A positive ion
A negative ion
It stays neutral
It becomes a different element
items.explanation: Gaining electrons adds negative charge, so the atom becomes a negative ion. Losing electrons (not gaining) makes a positive ion (choice 1); gaining electrons unbalances the charge, so it is no longer neutral (choice 3); and since the protons are unchanged, it is still the same element (choice 4).

items.prompt: When an atom becomes an ion, which part of it changes?
choices: The number of protons
The number of neutrons
The number of nuclei
The number of electrons
items.explanation: Only the number of electrons changes when an atom becomes an ion. The proton count never changes for an ion (choice 1) — thinking it does is a common error — neutrons are not what make an ion (choice 2), and the atom still has one nucleus (choice 3).

items.prompt: A neutral atom with 3 protons and 3 electrons loses 1 electron. What is true now?
choices: It now has 2 protons and is a new element
It now has 3 protons and 2 electrons, and is a positive ion
It now has 3 protons and 4 electrons, and is a negative ion
It is still neutral with 3 protons and 3 electrons
items.explanation: Losing 1 electron leaves 3 protons and 2 electrons, so there is one more + than − — a positive ion. The protons stay at 3, so it is the same element, not a new one (choice 1); losing an electron lowers the electron count to 2, not raises it to 4 (choice 3); and with unequal protons and electrons it is no longer neutral (choice 4).

lessons.title: Elements, Symbols, and the Great Map
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You already know that an atom is defined by its protons: count the protons and you know the element. But how do chemists keep track of *all* the elements at once? They use one famous chart — the **periodic table** — which works like a seating chart or a well-organized library, putting every element in its place so patterns appear.

**Build.** First, a recap. An **element** is one kind of atom: every atom of that element has the **same number of protons**. There are about **118 known elements**, and astonishingly, *every* material around you — water, air, metal, your own body — is built from these same 118 building blocks.

To handle them all, each element gets a short **symbol**: a one- or two-letter shorthand used by chemists worldwide. **H** is hydrogen, **O** is oxygen, **C** is carbon. A few symbols look surprising because they come from older, often Latin, names — that is why **sodium** is **Na** and **iron** is **Fe**. Same element, just an older name behind the letters.

Now the chart itself. The **periodic table** is the organized map of all the elements. Each element sits in its own box, or **cell**. A cell shows three things: the **symbol**, the **atomic number** (the number of protons, the element's ID), and the **atomic mass** (roughly how heavy the atom is). So one glance at a cell tells you what the element is and how heavy it runs.

**Recap.** An element is one kind of atom (fixed proton count); there are about 118 of them, and everything is built from them. Each has a 1–2 letter symbol (some from Latin names). The periodic table is the organized map, and each cell shows a symbol, an atomic number (protons), and an atomic mass.

items.prompt: What is the periodic table?
choices: The organized chart, or 'map', of all the known elements
A random pile of element names with no particular order
A list of mixtures you can make in a kitchen
Another word for a single atom
items.explanation: The periodic table is the organized chart of all the elements, arranged so patterns appear. It is not random or unordered (choice 1) — that is a common misconception — it lists elements, not mixtures (choice 3), and it shows every element, not just one atom (choice 4).

items.prompt: About how many elements are known, and what is built from them?
choices: About 118 elements, and every material around us is built from them
About 10 elements, and only metals are built from them
Exactly 26 elements, one for each letter of the alphabet
Thousands of elements, one for every material that exists
items.explanation: There are about 118 known elements, and every material — water, air, your body — is built from these. There are far more than 10 (choice 2), the count is not tied to the alphabet (choice 3), and materials are combinations of the ~118 elements, not a separate element each (choice 4).

items.prompt: An element is best described as:
choices: Any mixture of several different substances
A box on the table that is always empty
A particle smaller than a proton
One kind of atom, where every atom has the same number of protons
items.explanation: An element is one kind of atom: all its atoms share the same proton count, which is its identity. It is a single kind of stuff, not a mixture (choice 1); every element fills a real cell, never an empty box (choice 2); and an element is made of atoms, not something smaller than a proton (choice 3).

items.prompt: What is an element symbol, such as H or O?
choices: A made-up code that means nothing in particular
The number of protons written as a letter
A symbol that only works in one country
A short 1–2 letter shorthand for the element, used worldwide
items.explanation: A symbol is a one- or two-letter shorthand for an element, used by chemists everywhere. It is meaningful, not arbitrary (choice 1); it is letters standing for a name, not a proton count (choice 2); and it is an international standard, not country-specific (choice 3).

items.prompt: Why is the symbol for sodium 'Na' instead of 'So'?
choices: Because the symbol comes from an older, Latin name for the element
Because sodium is not really an element
Because symbols are chosen at random each year
Because 'Na' stands for the number of protons in sodium
items.explanation: Some symbols, like Na for sodium and Fe for iron, come from older (often Latin) names. Sodium is a real element (choice 2), symbols are fixed and not random (choice 3), and 'Na' is a name shorthand, not a proton count (choice 4).

items.prompt: Comparing the periodic table to a 'seating chart where similar people sit together' is meant to show that:
choices: The elements are placed in alphabetical order by name
The table is arranged so that similar elements end up grouped together
Elements can stand up and move around the chart
Only a few elements are given a seat
items.explanation: Like a seating chart that puts similar people together, the table is arranged so similar elements sit together and patterns appear. It is not alphabetical (choice 1) — a common wrong guess — elements stay in fixed places (choice 3), and every known element has a seat (choice 4).

items.prompt: A single cell of the periodic table shows which three things?
choices: The element's color, smell, and price
The number of mixtures the element can make
The symbol, the atomic number (protons), and the atomic mass
Three different symbols for the same element
items.explanation: A cell shows the symbol, the atomic number (proton count), and the atomic mass. It does not list color, smell, or price (choice 1) or a count of mixtures (choice 2), and each element has just one symbol, not three (choice 4).

items.prompt: On a table cell, the atomic number tells you:
choices: The number of protons, which is the element's identity
The temperature of the element
How many letters are in the symbol
The number of mixtures the element appears in
items.explanation: The atomic number is the number of protons — the element's ID, exactly as we learned for atoms. It is not a temperature (choice 2), it has nothing to do with how many letters the symbol has (choice 3), and it is not a count of mixtures (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which set correctly matches symbols to element names?
choices: H = helium, O = gold, C = copper
H = hydrogen, Na = nitrogen, Fe = fluorine
H = hydrogen, O = oxygen, Fe = iron
All elements share the single symbol 'E'
items.explanation: H is hydrogen, O is oxygen, and Fe is iron (from its Latin name). Choice 1 mismatches every pair, choice 2 wrongly reads Na as nitrogen (Na is sodium) and Fe as fluorine, and each element has its own symbol rather than a shared 'E' (choice 4).

lessons.title: How the Table Is Organized
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A map is only useful if you know how it is laid out. The periodic table has a clear plan: elements are placed in a fixed order, and *where* an element sits already tells you a lot about it. Let us learn how to read the layout.

**Build.** The elements are listed in order of **increasing atomic number** — that is, by **proton count**, starting at 1 and counting up. So the order is never alphabetical and never random; it follows the protons.

The table is a grid, so it has rows and columns. The horizontal **rows** are called **periods**. The vertical **columns** are called **groups** (also called **families**). Here is the key idea, and it is the whole point of the table: elements in the **same group (column) behave in similar ways** — they have similar chemistry. (The deeper reason, coming in a later module, is that they share the same number of outer electrons.) So a group is like a family whose members all act alike. Watch the trap: it is the **columns**, not the rows, that hold these look-alike families.

The layout also sorts elements by *type*. **Most elements are metals**: shiny, bendable, and good at carrying heat and electricity — think iron, copper, gold. They fill the left and center. On the right sit the **nonmetals**: dull, poor conductors, often gases or brittle solids — like oxygen, carbon, and sulfur. Right along the staircase border between them are the **metalloids**, the in-betweeners, such as silicon.

**Recap.** Elements are ordered by increasing atomic number (protons). Rows are periods; columns are groups/families, and same-column elements act alike. Most elements are metals (left/center); nonmetals are on the right; metalloids line the staircase between them.

items.prompt: Elements on the periodic table are listed in order of:
choices: Alphabetical order by name
How expensive each element is
No order at all — they are placed randomly
Increasing atomic number (proton count)
items.explanation: Elements are arranged by increasing atomic number, the proton count, starting at 1. They are not alphabetical (choice 1) — a frequent misconception — not by price (choice 2), and definitely not random (choice 3); the order follows the protons.

items.prompt: On the periodic table, the horizontal rows are called:
choices: Groups
Families
Periods
Cells
items.explanation: The horizontal rows are called periods. Groups and families (choices 1 and 2) are both names for the vertical columns, not the rows, and a cell (choice 4) is a single box for one element.

items.prompt: The vertical columns of the table, which hold the look-alike families, are called:
choices: Groups (or families)
Periods
Rows
Mixtures
items.explanation: The vertical columns are groups, also called families, and they hold elements that behave alike. Periods and rows (choices 2 and 3) are the horizontal lines, and a mixture (choice 4) is several substances together, not part of the table's layout.

items.prompt: Why does it matter that two elements are in the same group (column)?
choices: Because they will have the same color
Because they must weigh exactly the same
Because they are actually the same element
Because they tend to behave in similar ways — similar chemistry
items.explanation: Elements in the same group behave similarly — that shared chemistry is the whole point of the table. Same group does not mean same color (choice 1) or same weight (choice 2), and they are still different elements with different proton counts (choice 3).

items.prompt: A student says, 'Elements in the same ROW behave alike, like a family.' What is the correct fix?
choices: The student is right — rows are the look-alike families
It is the elements in the same COLUMN (group) that behave alike
No elements ever behave alike
Only elements that are alphabetically next to each other behave alike
items.explanation: The look-alike families run down the columns (groups), not across the rows (periods) — mixing these up is a classic error. So the student is not right (choice 1); plenty of elements behave alike within a column (choice 3); and behavior follows the column, not alphabetical order (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which statement about how many elements are metals is correct?
choices: Every single element is a metal
Most elements are metals, found on the left and center of the table
No elements are metals
Only the elements on the far right are metals
items.explanation: Most elements are metals, sitting on the left and center of the table. It is not true that every element is a metal (choice 1) — nonmetals and metalloids exist too — metals certainly do exist (choice 3), and metals are on the left/center, while the far right holds nonmetals (choice 4).

items.prompt: Copper is shiny, bendable, and carries electricity well (think copper wire). Copper is a:
choices: Nonmetal
Metalloid
Metal
Mixture
items.explanation: Shiny, bendable, and a good conductor are the signs of a metal, so copper is a metal. A nonmetal (choice 1) would be dull and a poor conductor; a metalloid (choice 2) is an in-betweener like silicon; and a mixture (choice 4) is not a single element at all.

items.prompt: Oxygen is a gas you breathe; it is dull (not shiny) and a poor conductor. Oxygen is a:
choices: Metal
Nonmetal
Metalloid
Type of metal because all elements are metals
items.explanation: Dull, a poor conductor, and a gas are the hallmarks of a nonmetal, so oxygen is a nonmetal. It is not shiny and conducting like a metal (choice 1), not a staircase in-betweener like a metalloid (choice 3), and the idea that all elements are metals is simply false (choice 4).

items.prompt: What are metalloids?
choices: Another name for the most reactive metals
Elements that have no atomic number
Elements with in-between properties, lying along the staircase border (e.g. silicon)
Mixtures of a metal and a nonmetal stirred together
items.explanation: Metalloids sit along the staircase border and have properties between metals and nonmetals — silicon is a familiar example. They are not the most reactive metals (choice 1), every element has an atomic number (choice 2), and a metalloid is a single element, not a stirred-together mixture (choice 4).

lessons.title: Famous Families and the Story the Table Tells
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Now that you can read the layout, the table starts to *tell a story*. A few columns are so distinctive that they have famous names and well-known personalities. Learn these, and the table begins to predict how elements behave — without memorizing 118 separate facts.

**Build.** Picture the columns as families with personalities. On the **far left** are the **alkali metals**. They are **super reactive** — so eager to react that they fizz and react with plain water. At the **far right** are the **noble gases**, like **helium** and **neon**. They are almost **totally unreactive** (the word for this is **inert**): they sit calmly and tend not to react with anything. Just inside the noble gases, one column to their left, are the **halogens** — **very reactive nonmetals**, such as **chlorine**. (Exactly *why* some families are reactive and others are not is the next module's story; for now, just learn the personalities.)

Here is the big payoff: **position predicts behavior**. Where an element sits on the table hints at how it will act — whether it is a metal, whether it is reactive, which family it belongs to. The far-left column screams 'reactive metal', the far-right column whispers 'leave me alone'. You do not memorize 118 facts; you **read the map**, and the map does the predicting.

So the table is more than a list. It is a story written in rows and columns: each position carries meaning, and similar elements line up so their shared 'personalities' become visible at a glance.

**Recap.** Alkali metals (far left) are super reactive; noble gases (far right, e.g. helium, neon) are inert/unreactive; halogens (next to the noble gases) are very reactive nonmetals like chlorine. Position predicts behavior — you read the map instead of memorizing every element.

items.prompt: The noble gases, such as helium and neon, are best described as:
choices: Extremely reactive, exploding on contact with water
Almost totally unreactive (inert)
The most common metals on the table
Mixtures rather than elements
items.explanation: Noble gases are almost totally unreactive, or inert — that is their whole personality. Thinking they are extremely reactive (choice 1) is a common mistake; they are nonmetal gases, not metals (choice 3); and they are elements, not mixtures (choice 4).

items.prompt: Where on the table are the noble gases found?
choices: The far-right column
The far-left column
Scattered randomly all over the table
Only in the middle rows
items.explanation: The noble gases occupy the far-right column. The far-left column (choice 2) holds the reactive alkali metals instead; the table is organized, not random (choice 3); and the noble gases form a specific column, not the middle rows (choice 4).

items.prompt: The alkali metals, on the far-left column, are known for being:
choices: Completely unreactive, like the noble gases
Unable to be found anywhere on the table
The only nonmetals on the table
Super reactive — reactive enough to react with water
items.explanation: Alkali metals are super reactive, reacting even with plain water — the opposite of the inert noble gases. They are not unreactive (choice 1), they sit in the far-left column rather than being absent (choice 2), and they are metals, not nonmetals (choice 3).

items.prompt: Chlorine belongs to the halogens. The halogens are:
choices: Unreactive gases that never react
Soft, reactive metals on the far left
Very reactive nonmetals, sitting next to the noble gases
Metalloids found only on the staircase
items.explanation: The halogens, like chlorine, are very reactive nonmetals located just next to the noble gases. They are not unreactive (choice 1) — that describes the noble gases — they are nonmetals, not far-left metals (choice 2), and they are not metalloids (choice 4).

items.prompt: A friend claims, 'Noble gases like helium are some of the MOST reactive elements.' Why is this wrong?
choices: It is correct — noble gases are the most reactive
Noble gases are actually almost unreactive (inert); the alkali metals are the very reactive ones
Helium is not a real element
Reactivity has nothing to do with the periodic table
items.explanation: Noble gases are nearly inert, the opposite of reactive — the very reactive elements include the alkali metals on the far left. So the claim is not correct (choice 1); helium is a real element (choice 3); and reactivity is closely tied to an element's position on the table (choice 4).

items.prompt: What does it mean that 'position predicts behavior' on the periodic table?
choices: Elements physically move to predict the weather
You must memorize all 118 elements one by one anyway
An element's position tells you its exact price
Where an element sits hints at how it behaves — whether it is a metal, how reactive it is
items.explanation: An element's position hints at its behavior — its metal/nonmetal nature, its reactivity, its family. The phrase is not about weather (choice 1); the whole point is that you read the map instead of memorizing 118 facts (choice 2); and position predicts chemistry, not price (choice 3).

items.prompt: An element sits in the far-LEFT column of the table. From its position, you can guess it is:
choices: An inert noble gas
A super-reactive alkali metal
A metalloid on the staircase
Impossible to guess anything about
items.explanation: The far-left column is the alkali metals, so position alone suggests a super-reactive metal. The inert noble gases are on the far right, not the left (choice 1); metalloids line the staircase, not the far-left column (choice 3); and the whole lesson is that position does let you make a good guess (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why is the periodic table described as 'telling a story' rather than being just a list?
choices: Because the boxes are arranged in alphabetical order like a dictionary
Because each position carries meaning, so the layout itself reveals patterns and predicts behavior
Because the table is a random jumble with no pattern
Because it lists mixtures instead of elements
items.explanation: Each position carries meaning, so the arrangement reveals patterns and lets you predict behavior — that is the 'story'. The table is not alphabetical (choice 1) or random (choice 3), and it lists elements, not mixtures (choice 4).

items.prompt: Helium is a noble gas (it fills floating balloons). Based on its family, helium is:
choices: Very reactive, like an alkali metal
Almost unreactive (inert), like the other noble gases
A shiny, bendable metal
A metalloid on the staircase border
items.explanation: As a noble gas, helium shares that family's inert personality — almost unreactive. It is not reactive like an alkali metal (choice 1), it is a gas rather than a shiny metal (choice 3), and noble gases are not the staircase metalloids (choice 4).

lessons.title: Electron Shells: Layers Around the Nucleus
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You already know electrons are the tiny negative (−) particles that orbit the nucleus. Here is the next surprise: they are not all crammed at the same distance. They sit in neat **layers**, and those layers are the secret to why every atom behaves the way it does.

**Build.** Each layer of electrons is called a **shell** (also called an **energy level**). Picture the layers of an **onion**, or rows of seats in circles around a stage: some electrons sit close to the nucleus, others sit farther out. A shell is just one of those layers.

Two simple rules tell you how the shells fill. First, **inner shells fill first** — electrons fill from the **inside out**, like filling the front rows of seats before the back rows. Second, **each shell holds a limited number**. For the small atoms we care about, the **first (innermost) shell holds only 2** electrons, and the **next shell holds up to 8**. So an atom puts 2 electrons in the first shell, and only once that is full does it start filling the second.

One honest note: electrons do not really run on neat circular tracks. They live in fuzzy **'clouds'** — regions where they are likely to be. But the layered-shell picture is a fine, useful model, and it is all you need. (No math here — just layers that fill from the inside out.)

**Recap.** Electrons sit in layers called **shells** (energy levels) around the nucleus, like onion layers. Inner shells fill first; the first shell holds 2 and the next holds up to 8. The neat rings are a handy model for fuzzy electron clouds.

items.prompt: An electron 'shell' (also called an energy level) is best described as:
choices: Another name for the nucleus at the center of the atom
A single proton found in the middle of the atom
A hard outer wall that traps the whole atom inside
A layer, at a certain distance from the nucleus, where electrons sit
items.explanation: A shell is a layer of electrons at a certain distance from the nucleus — like one layer of an onion. It is not the nucleus (choice 1), which holds protons and neutrons; it is not a proton (choice 2); and it is not a solid wall (choice 3) — it is simply where some electrons sit.

items.prompt: Are all of an atom's electrons the same distance from the nucleus?
choices: Yes — every electron sits at exactly the same distance
No — they sit in layers (shells), some closer and some farther out
Yes — they are all packed inside the nucleus together
No — but only because they are all outside the atom entirely
items.explanation: Electrons sit in layers called shells, so some are closer to the nucleus and some are farther out. They are not all at one distance (choice 1); they are not inside the nucleus (choice 3), which holds only protons and neutrons; and they are part of the atom, not outside it (choice 4).

items.prompt: In which order do the shells fill with electrons?
choices: The outer shells fill first, then the inner ones
They all fill at once, evenly, with the same number each
From the inside out — the innermost shell fills first
In a completely random order that changes each time
items.explanation: Electrons fill from the inside out: the innermost shell fills first, like taking the front-row seats first. The outer shells do not fill first (choice 1); the shells do not all fill at once or hold the same number (choice 2); and the order is fixed, not random (choice 4).

items.prompt: How many electrons does the first (innermost) shell hold?
choices: It can hold any number, with no limit
Exactly the same number as every other shell
items.explanation: The first shell holds just 2 electrons. 8 is the amount the next shell holds, not the first (choice 1); a shell does have a limit, not unlimited room (choice 2); and shells do not all hold the same number — thinking they do is a common mistake (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'Every shell holds exactly the same number of electrons.' Why is this wrong?
choices: It is actually right — all shells hold the same number
Because shells hold different limits — for small atoms the first holds 2 and the next holds up to 8
Because shells cannot hold any electrons at all
Because only the nucleus can hold electrons
items.explanation: Different shells have different limits: the first holds 2 and the next holds up to 8, so they are not all the same. The claim is therefore wrong (choice 1); shells do hold electrons (choice 3); and electrons sit in the shells, not in the nucleus (choice 4).

items.prompt: The comparison of electron shells to 'the layers of an onion' is meant to show that:
choices: Atoms smell strong, just like onions do
Electrons are arranged in layers, one inside the next, around the center
You can peel electrons off an atom by hand
An atom has exactly the same size as an onion
items.explanation: Like onion layers nested one inside the next, electron shells are layers around the nucleus. The analogy is not about smell (choice 1), it is not a claim that you peel electrons by hand (choice 3), and it says nothing about an atom's real size (choice 4).

items.prompt: An atom has 2 electrons in the first shell. Where do its next electrons go?
choices: They squeeze into the first shell, which has no limit
Into the nucleus, next to the protons
They leave the atom completely
Into the second shell, since the first is now full
items.explanation: The first shell is full at 2, so the next electrons go into the second shell — fill from the inside out. The first shell does have a limit, so they cannot just squeeze in (choice 1); electrons do not go into the nucleus (choice 2); and they stay part of the atom rather than leaving (choice 3).

items.prompt: We draw electrons on tidy rings, but what is the more honest picture of where they are?
choices: They sit perfectly still, glued to one fixed spot
They are packed solidly inside the nucleus
They live in fuzzy 'clouds' — regions where they are likely to be — and rings are just a useful model
They are actually solid metal tracks you could touch
items.explanation: Electrons really live in fuzzy clouds, regions where they are likely to be found; the neat rings are just a handy model. They are not frozen in one spot (choice 1), not inside the nucleus (choice 2), and the rings are a drawing aid, not real solid tracks (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why is it correct to say an atom fills its first shell before it uses the second?
