choices: Because electrons fill from the inside out, and inner shells fill first
Because the second shell is always broken and unusable
Because the outer shell is the only one electrons ever use
Because the first shell can hold an unlimited number of electrons
items.explanation: Electrons fill from the inside out, so the inner first shell fills before the second is used. The second shell is perfectly usable once the first is full (choice 2); atoms use inner shells too, not only the outer one (choice 3); and the first shell is limited to 2, not unlimited (choice 4).

lessons.title: Valence Electrons and the Drive to Fill the Shell
lessons.content:
**Hook.** An atom may have many electrons, but only a few of them ever do anything interesting. Which ones? The ones on the **outside**. This single idea — that the outer electrons run the show — is the heart of all chemistry.

**Build.** The electrons in the **outermost shell** are called the **valence electrons**, and they are the ones that **interact with other atoms**. The inner electrons mostly just sit there, tucked away. Think of yourself in a crowd: only your **outermost layer** — your skin, your coat — actually touches the world; what is underneath stays hidden. In the same way, only the outer-shell electrons reach out and touch other atoms. So the key beginner idea is simple: **chemistry is mostly about outer-shell (valence) electrons.**

Now, why should an atom bother reacting at all? Because atoms are most **stable** — most 'content' — when their **outer shell is full** (2 for the first shell, **8** for the next ones; this 8 is famous enough to have a name, the **octet**). An atom with an unfilled outer shell behaves as if it 'wants' to fill it. So atoms **react in order to reach a full outer shell** — by **gaining, losing, or sharing** electrons. It is like completing a set or filling the last empty seats: once the outer shell is full, the atom is satisfied. This drive to fill the outer shell is the **engine behind all bonding**, which is the next module's story.

**Recap.** **Valence electrons** are the outer-shell electrons, and they do the chemistry; inner electrons mostly sit still. Atoms are most stable with a **full outer shell** (the octet, 8), and the drive to fill it — by gaining, losing, or sharing electrons — is **why atoms react**.

items.prompt: Valence electrons are:
choices: The electrons packed in the innermost shell
The protons found in the nucleus
The electrons in the outermost shell of the atom
Any electron that has no charge
items.explanation: Valence electrons are the ones in the outermost shell. They are not the innermost electrons (choice 1); they are electrons, not protons (choice 2); and all electrons carry a negative charge, so 'no charge' is wrong (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which electrons actually do the chemistry — the ones that interact with other atoms?
choices: The inner-shell electrons, since they are closest to the nucleus
The outer-shell (valence) electrons
The neutrons in the outer shell
None of them — electrons never take part in chemistry
items.explanation: The outer-shell (valence) electrons are the ones that reach other atoms and do the chemistry. Inner electrons mostly just sit there, so choice 1 is the classic mistake; neutrons are not electrons and are not in shells (choice 3); and electrons are exactly what drives chemistry (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'It is the inner electrons, deep inside, that react with other atoms.' What is the correct fix?
choices: The student is right — inner electrons do the reacting
It is the outer-shell (valence) electrons that interact; inner electrons mostly just sit there
Neither inner nor outer electrons ever react
Only the nucleus reacts with other atoms
items.explanation: The outer (valence) electrons are the ones that interact with other atoms, while the inner electrons mostly stay put. So the student is wrong (choice 1); electrons certainly do react (choice 3); and the nucleus stays out of ordinary chemistry (choice 4).

items.prompt: When is an atom most stable (most 'content')?
choices: When its outer shell is completely empty
When it has lost its nucleus
When its outer shell is completely full
When all of its electrons are in the first shell only
items.explanation: An atom is most stable when its outer shell is full. Reacting to EMPTY the shell is a backwards idea (choice 1); an atom cannot lose its nucleus and stay that atom (choice 2); and electrons cannot all crowd into the first shell, which only holds 2 (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why do atoms react in the first place?
choices: To empty their outer shell of all electrons
To get rid of their nucleus
To reach a full outer shell (by gaining, losing, or sharing electrons)
For no reason — reactions just happen at random
items.explanation: Atoms react in order to reach a full outer shell, by gaining, losing, or sharing electrons. They are not trying to empty the shell (choice 1) — a common backwards belief — they keep their nucleus (choice 2), and reacting is driven by the full-shell goal, not pure chance (choice 4).

items.prompt: The 'octet' refers to which number of electrons filling an outer shell?
items.explanation: The octet is 8 — a full outer shell for the early elements beyond the first shell. 2 is the amount that fills the very first shell, not the octet (choice 1); 118 is roughly the number of known elements (choice 2); and 0 would be an empty shell, the opposite of full (choice 3).

items.prompt: What are the three ways an atom can change its electrons to reach a full outer shell?
choices: Gaining, losing, or sharing electrons
Heating, freezing, or melting its electrons
Gaining, losing, or sharing protons
Painting, weighing, or counting its electrons
items.explanation: An atom reaches a full outer shell by gaining, losing, or sharing electrons — the three moves behind all bonding. Heating or freezing is about temperature, not shells (choice 2); protons stay fixed (changing them would change the element), so choice 3 is wrong; and painting or weighing are not real options (choice 4).

items.prompt: The idea that an atom 'wants a full outer shell' is compared to:
choices: Throwing away every item you own
Completing a set or filling the last empty seats
Tearing a finished puzzle apart
Making sure a container stays completely empty
items.explanation: Filling the outer shell is like completing a set or filling the last empty seats — the atom is satisfied once it is full. It is not about throwing things away (choice 1) or tearing a finished set apart (choice 3), and the goal is a full shell, not an empty one (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why are valence electrons compared to your 'outermost layer, like your skin or coat'?
choices: Because only the outermost part touches the world — just as only outer electrons touch other atoms
Because electrons keep you warm like a coat does
Because your skin is made of valence electrons
Because inner electrons are the only ones that matter
items.explanation: Only your outermost layer touches the outside world, and likewise only the outer (valence) electrons touch other atoms. The analogy is not about warmth (choice 2) or a literal claim about skin (choice 3), and it shows the OUTER electrons matter most, not the inner ones (choice 4).

items.prompt: This drive to fill the outer shell is described as the 'engine behind' what?
choices: Changing one element into another
All bonding — how atoms stick together
Making atoms heavier by adding neutrons
Giving atoms their color
items.explanation: The drive to fill the outer shell is the engine behind all bonding — the next module's topic. It does not change one element into another (that needs a proton change), so choice 1 is wrong; it is about electrons, not added neutrons (choice 3); and it is not about color (choice 4).

lessons.title: Noble Gases, Families, and Predicting Bonds
lessons.content:
**Hook.** In the periodic-table sub-topic you learned two facts and had to take them on faith: noble gases are **unreactive**, and elements in the **same column** behave alike. Now you can finally see **why** — and it all comes down to valence electrons.

**Build.** Start with the **noble gases** (like helium and neon). They are **inert** — almost totally unreactive — and now the reason is obvious: their outer shell is **already full**. With a full outer shell, an atom has **no drive to react**; it is already content, so it leaves other atoms alone. That is the whole secret behind their calm personality.

Next, why do **same-column elements act alike**? Because elements in one column have the **same number of valence (outer) electrons**. Since chemistry is run by the outer electrons, the same outer-electron count gives the **same 'personality'** — similar reactions. That closes the loop from before: the columns line up elements that share an outer-electron count, so they behave the same way.

Finally, valence electrons let you **predict how atoms combine**. An atom with **1 outer electron** tends to **lose** that one; an atom **needing just 1 more** tends to **gain or share** one. So they fit together: **sodium loses 1** electron and **chlorine gains 1**, and the two pair up neatly. (This is just a preview — full bonding is the next module.)

**Recap.** Noble gases are unreactive because their outer shell is **already full** (no drive to react). Same-column elements behave alike because they have the **same number of valence electrons**. And valence electrons **predict bonding**: an atom with 1 outer electron (sodium) pairs with one needing 1 more (chlorine).

items.prompt: Why are the noble gases (like helium and neon) almost completely unreactive?
choices: Because their outer shell is completely empty
Because they have no electrons at all
Because they are the heaviest elements on the table
Because their outer shell is already full, so they have no drive to react
items.explanation: Noble gases already have a full outer shell, so they have no drive to react — that is why they are inert. Their outer shell is full, not empty (choice 1); they do have electrons (choice 2); and being unreactive is about a full shell, not about weight (choice 3).

items.prompt: A friend insists, 'Noble gases are some of the MOST reactive elements.' Why is this wrong?
choices: It is correct — noble gases react with almost everything
Because their outer shell is already full, so they are inert and have no drive to react
Because noble gases are not real elements
Because reactivity has nothing to do with electrons
items.explanation: A full outer shell means no drive to react, so noble gases are inert, not reactive — thinking they are highly reactive is a classic error. They are real elements (choice 3), and reactivity is all about the outer electrons (choice 4), so the claim in choice 1 is simply backwards.

items.prompt: Why do elements in the same column (group) of the periodic table behave in similar ways?
choices: Because they weigh exactly the same amount
Because they have the same number of valence (outer-shell) electrons
Because they have the same number of neutrons
Because the outer-electron count has nothing to do with which column an element is in
items.explanation: Same-column elements share the same number of valence electrons, and since outer electrons run the chemistry, they behave alike. It is not about equal weight (choice 1) or equal neutrons (choice 3); and choice 4 is the misconception this lesson corrects — the column is tied directly to the outer-electron count.

items.prompt: Two elements sit in the same column. What can you say about their outer (valence) electrons?
choices: They have the same number of outer electrons
One has outer electrons and the other has none
Their outer-electron counts are unrelated to the column
Neither one has any outer electrons
items.explanation: Elements in one column have the same number of outer electrons, which is why they act alike. It is not that one has them and the other does not (choice 2); the count is tied to the column, not unrelated to it (choice 3); and both have outer electrons (choice 4).

items.prompt: An atom has just 1 electron in its outer shell. To reach a stable full shell, it most easily tends to:
choices: Gain 7 more electrons all at once
Lose that 1 outer electron
Lose its nucleus
Do nothing, because it is already full
items.explanation: With only 1 outer electron, the easiest path to a full shell is to lose that single electron. Gaining 7 is a far harder route (choice 1); an atom cannot lose its nucleus (choice 3); and a single outer electron is not a full shell, so 'do nothing' is wrong (choice 4).

items.prompt: An atom needs just 1 more electron to fill its outer shell. It most easily tends to:
choices: Lose 7 of its outer electrons
Gain or share 1 electron
Empty its outer shell entirely
Turn into a noble gas by changing its protons
items.explanation: Needing only 1 more, the atom most easily gains or shares a single electron to fill the shell. Dumping 7 electrons is the hard way (choice 1); emptying the shell is the opposite of filling it (choice 3); and an atom does not become a noble gas by changing protons — it fills its shell by adjusting electrons (choice 4).

items.prompt: Sodium has 1 outer electron and chlorine needs 1 more to fill its shell. Why do they pair up so neatly?
choices: Both atoms empty their outer shells together
Chlorine gives sodium a proton
Sodium gives away its 1 outer electron and chlorine takes the 1 it needs
They cannot combine, because outer electrons do not matter
items.explanation: Sodium loses its single outer electron and chlorine gains the one it needs, so each reaches a full shell — a perfect match. They are filling shells, not emptying them (choice 1); no protons are exchanged (choice 2); and outer electrons are exactly what makes the pairing work (choice 4).

items.prompt: How do valence electrons help predict how an atom will combine with others?
choices: They reveal the exact color the atom will turn
The outer-electron count shows whether an atom tends to lose, gain, or share electrons to fill its shell
They tell you how many neutrons the atom will gain
They predict nothing — combining is purely random
items.explanation: The number of valence electrons tells you whether an atom tends to lose, gain, or share electrons, which predicts how it combines. It is not about color (choice 1) or gaining neutrons (choice 3), and combining follows the outer-electron count rather than being random (choice 4).

items.prompt: A noble gas has a full outer shell; a reactive atom has an unfilled outer shell. Which is which?
choices: The full-shell atom is reactive; the unfilled-shell atom is content
Both are equally reactive, because the shell does not matter
The full-shell atom is content (unreactive); the unfilled-shell atom is reactive
Neither one will ever react under any conditions
items.explanation: A full outer shell means content and unreactive (the noble gas), while an unfilled shell means reactive (it still 'wants' to fill up). Choice 1 reverses this; the shell absolutely matters, so choice 2 is wrong; and reactive atoms clearly do react, making choice 4 wrong.

subtopics.title: Chemical Bonds: How Atoms Stick Together

lessons.title: What a Chemical Bond Is: Atoms Team Up
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Why do atoms ever stick together at all? Why isn't the world just a fog of separate, lonely atoms? Because atoms can lock onto each other and stay joined. That join has a name: a **chemical bond**.

**Build.** A **chemical bond** is simply the **force that holds two atoms together**. Think of it as the 'glue' between atoms. But what makes the glue? Recall the last module: atoms are most stable when their **outer shell** (their outermost layer of electrons) is **full**, and an unfilled outer shell makes an atom 'want' to fill up. A bond is how atoms get there. Atoms **team up** so that each one can reach a full set of outer electrons — like classmates pairing off so nobody is left short a partner.

There are two basic moves to pull this off, and they lead to the **three main kinds** of bond. An atom can **transfer** electrons (give or take them) — that gives an **ionic** bond. Or atoms can **share** electrons — that gives a **covalent** bond. And in a chunk of metal, many atoms pool their outer electrons together — that is a **metallic** bond. So: **ionic, covalent, metallic**. We will meet each one in turn.

The key idea to hold onto: atoms do not bond for no reason. They bond to reach **full, stable outer shells** — by transferring or sharing electrons.

**Recap.** A **chemical bond** is the force that holds atoms together, and atoms bond to reach **full outer shells** by transferring or sharing electrons. The three main kinds are **ionic, covalent, and metallic**.

items.prompt: What is a chemical bond?
choices: The force that holds two atoms together
A tiny particle found inside the nucleus
The empty space between two atoms that keeps them apart
Another name for a single electron
items.explanation: A chemical bond is the force that holds atoms together, the 'glue' between them. It is not a particle in the nucleus (choice 2); it pulls atoms together rather than keeping them apart (choice 3); and it is a force, not an electron itself (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why do atoms form bonds in the first place?
choices: To empty their outer shell of all electrons
To reach a full, stable outer shell
To get rid of their nucleus
For no reason at all; bonding is purely random
items.explanation: Atoms bond to reach a full, stable outer shell, by transferring or sharing electrons. Bonding fills the outer shell, it does not empty it, so choice 1 is a common backwards idea; atoms keep their nucleus (choice 3); and bonding follows the full-shell goal, not chance (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'Atoms bond in order to EMPTY their outer shell.' What is the correct fix?
choices: The student is right; atoms bond to empty the outer shell
Atoms bond to lose their nucleus, not to change electrons
Atoms bond to reach a FULL outer shell, not to empty it
Atoms never bond, so the question makes no sense
items.explanation: The goal of bonding is a FULL outer shell, the opposite of empty. So the student has it backwards (choice 1); bonding involves electrons, and atoms keep their nucleus (choice 2); and atoms do bond all the time (choice 4).

items.prompt: What are the three main kinds of chemical bond named in this lesson?
choices: Solid, liquid, and gas
Proton, neutron, and electron
Hot, cold, and warm
Ionic, covalent, and metallic
items.explanation: The three main kinds of bond are ionic, covalent, and metallic. Solid, liquid, and gas are states of matter, not bonds (choice 1); proton, neutron, and electron are parts of an atom (choice 2); and hot, cold, and warm describe temperature (choice 3).

items.prompt: The two basic moves that let atoms reach a full outer shell are:
choices: Transferring electrons or sharing electrons
Heating electrons or freezing electrons
Transferring protons or sharing protons
Painting electrons or counting electrons
items.explanation: Atoms reach a full outer shell either by transferring electrons (giving or taking them) or by sharing electrons. Heating or freezing is about temperature, not bonding (choice 2); protons stay fixed in the nucleus (choice 3); and painting or counting are not real chemistry moves (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best fits why atoms bond?
choices: Atoms throwing away every electron they own
Atoms teaming up so each one gets a full set of outer electrons
Atoms making sure their outer shell stays completely empty
Atoms tearing a finished set apart on purpose
items.explanation: Bonding is like atoms teaming up so each gets a full set of outer electrons, like classmates pairing off so nobody is left short. It is not about throwing electrons away (choice 1), keeping a shell empty (choice 3), or breaking a finished set apart (choice 4).

items.prompt: The 'outer shell' that an atom is trying to fill by bonding is:
choices: The nucleus at the very center of the atom
A hard wall around the outside of the atom
The outermost layer of electrons around the atom
A type of bond between two atoms
items.explanation: The outer shell is the outermost layer of electrons, the one whose electrons (valence electrons) do the chemistry. It is not the nucleus (choice 1), not a solid wall (choice 2), and not a bond itself (choice 4) but the thing a bond helps to fill.

items.prompt: If there were NO chemical bonds at all, what would the world be like?
choices: Exactly the same as it is now
Just separate, lonely atoms that never join into anything
Made only of metals and nothing else
Full of atoms with empty nuclei
items.explanation: Bonds are what join atoms into everything around us, so without them there would only be separate, lonely atoms. The world clearly would not be the same (choice 1); metals also need bonds to hold together (choice 3); and bonds are about electrons, not emptying nuclei (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which statement best sums up this lesson?
choices: A bond is a particle, and atoms bond to gain neutrons
A bond is the force holding atoms together, and atoms bond to reach full outer shells
A bond only exists in metals, and nowhere else
Atoms bond to change one element into another
items.explanation: A chemical bond is the force that holds atoms together, and atoms bond to reach full, stable outer shells. A bond is a force, not a particle, and bonding is about electrons not neutrons (choice 1); bonds appear in many materials, not just metals (choice 3); and bonding does not change the element, which would require changing protons (choice 4).

lessons.title: Ionic and Covalent: Giving vs Sharing Electrons
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Two atoms both need a full outer shell. They have two very different ways to get one: one atom can **give** an electron to the other, or the two can **share** electrons. These two paths are the first two kinds of bond.

**Build.** First, **ionic bonding**, the 'give' path. Here a **metal gives** one or more electrons to a **nonmetal**. (A metal is an element like sodium that lets go of outer electrons easily; a nonmetal, like chlorine, is hungry to gain them.) When the metal gives an electron away, it becomes a **positive ion** (a charged atom, written with a **+**); the nonmetal that gains the electron becomes a **negative ion** (**-**). And here is the magic: **opposite charges attract**, so the + and - pull toward each other and stay stuck, like two magnets. The classic example: **sodium gives 1 electron to chlorine**, making **table salt (NaCl)**. Ionic compounds line up into orderly **crystals**, are usually **brittle** solids, and often **dissolve in water**.

Now the 'share' path: **covalent bonding**. Here **two nonmetals share** electrons, so both atoms get to **count a full shell** from the same shared pair, like two people sharing a set of tools so each has a full kit. Two hydrogen atoms share to make **H2**; oxygen atoms, and **water (H2O)** and **carbon dioxide**, are held together this way. Shared electrons make a **molecule**, a distinct little unit.

The quick test: **metal + nonmetal usually means transfer (ionic); two nonmetals means share (covalent).**

**Recap.** **Ionic** = a metal **gives** electrons to a nonmetal, making + and - ions that **attract** (table salt). **Covalent** = two nonmetals **share** electrons, making molecules (H2, water). Transfer versus share.

items.prompt: In an IONIC bond, what happens to the electrons?
choices: They are shared equally between two atoms
One atom gives (transfers) electrons to another atom
They disappear completely
They turn into protons
items.explanation: In an ionic bond, electrons are transferred: a metal gives them to a nonmetal. Sharing is what happens in a COVALENT bond, not an ionic one, so choice 1 is the classic mix-up; electrons are not destroyed (choice 3); and electrons never turn into protons (choice 4).

items.prompt: After a metal gives an electron away in an ionic bond, what charge does that metal atom have?
choices: A negative charge (-)
No charge at all
A positive charge (+)
It becomes a neutron
items.explanation: Giving away a negative electron leaves the metal atom with a positive charge (+), so it becomes a positive ion. It does not become negative, which is what the nonmetal that GAINS the electron becomes (choice 1); losing an electron leaves an imbalance, so it is not neutral (choice 2); and an atom cannot become a neutron (choice 4).

items.prompt: What actually holds a positive ion and a negative ion together in an ionic bond?
choices: Opposite charges attract, so + and - pull together
Like charges attract, so + pulls another +
They share a pair of electrons equally
Nothing; the ions simply drift apart
items.explanation: Opposite charges attract, so the + ion and - ion pull toward each other and stick, like two magnets. Like charges actually repel, not attract (choice 2); sharing a pair describes a covalent bond, not ionic (choice 3); and the attraction keeps them together rather than drifting apart (choice 4).

items.prompt: Table salt (NaCl) forms when sodium and chlorine react. Which bond is this, and why?
choices: Covalent, because the two atoms share a pair of electrons
Ionic, because sodium (a metal) gives 1 electron to chlorine (a nonmetal)
Metallic, because both atoms pool electrons into a sea
No bond at all, because they just sit side by side
items.explanation: Sodium is a metal that gives 1 electron to chlorine, a nonmetal, so this is an ionic bond (metal + nonmetal = transfer). It is not covalent, since the electron is given away rather than shared (choice 1); it is not metallic (choice 3); and the + and - ions are firmly bonded, not just resting side by side (choice 4).

items.prompt: In a COVALENT bond, what do the two atoms do with electrons?
choices: One atom gives all its electrons to the other
They share electrons so both can count a full shell
They throw their electrons away to empty their shells
They swap their nuclei
items.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share electrons so both get to count a full outer shell. Giving electrons away describes an IONIC bond, not covalent, so choice 1 is the common transfer-vs-share mix-up; atoms aim to fill, not empty, their shells (choice 3); and nuclei are never swapped (choice 4).

items.prompt: Two hydrogen atoms join to make H2 by sharing a pair of electrons. Which bond is this?
choices: Ionic, because one hydrogen gives an electron to the other
Metallic, because hydrogen is a metal
Covalent, because the two atoms share electrons
No bond, because two of the same atom cannot join
items.explanation: Sharing a pair of electrons between two nonmetal atoms is a covalent bond, so H2 is covalent. It is not ionic, since nothing is given away here (choice 1); hydrogen is not a metal forming a sea of electrons (choice 2); and two identical atoms certainly can bond (choice 4).

items.prompt: A friend says, 'Ionic means sharing electrons, and covalent means giving them away.' What is the fix?
choices: The friend is correct as stated
It is backwards: ionic means GIVING (transfer), covalent means SHARING
Both ionic and covalent mean giving electrons away
Both ionic and covalent mean sharing electrons
items.explanation: It is exactly backwards: ionic is transfer (giving and taking), while covalent is sharing. So the friend has the two swapped (choice 1); the two bonds are different moves, so they cannot both be giving (choice 3) or both be sharing (choice 4).

items.prompt: Water (H2O) and carbon dioxide are held together by which kind of bond?
choices: Covalent bonds, because nonmetals share electrons
Ionic bonds, because a metal gives electrons to a nonmetal
Metallic bonds, because they conduct electricity
No bonds, because gases have no bonds
items.explanation: Water and carbon dioxide are made of nonmetals sharing electrons, so they are held by covalent bonds and form molecules. There is no metal giving electrons away here, so it is not ionic (choice 2); they are not metals with an electron sea (choice 3); and their atoms are firmly bonded into molecules (choice 4).

items.prompt: Quick rule: you have a metal bonding with a nonmetal. Which bond type do you expect?
choices: Covalent (sharing)
Ionic (transfer)
Metallic (electron sea)
It is impossible to tell
items.explanation: Metal + nonmetal usually means a transfer of electrons, which is an ionic bond. Covalent is for two nonmetals sharing (choice 1); metallic is for metal atoms pooling electrons among themselves (choice 3); and the metal-plus-nonmetal clue makes it quite predictable, not a mystery (choice 4).

items.prompt: You have two nonmetals bonding together. Which bond type do you expect?
choices: Ionic (transfer)
Metallic (electron sea)
Covalent (sharing)
No bond can form
items.explanation: Two nonmetals share electrons, which is a covalent bond. Ionic needs a metal to give electrons to a nonmetal (choice 1); metallic needs metal atoms pooling electrons (choice 2); and two nonmetals bond readily by sharing (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which of these is a typical property of an ionic compound like table salt?
choices: It is always a soft metal you can bend
It pools its electrons into a sea
It is a gas that never dissolves in anything
It forms orderly crystals and often dissolves in water
items.explanation: Ionic compounds such as salt form orderly crystals, are usually brittle, and often dissolve in water. They are not bendable metals (choice 2); salt is a solid that dissolves readily, not a gas that never dissolves (choice 3); and pooling electrons into a sea describes metallic bonding, not ionic (choice 4).

lessons.title: Unequal Sharing and the Metal's Sea of Electrons
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Sharing in a covalent bond is not always fair. And metals do something different from everything we have seen. Two final ideas finish the picture of bonding.

**Build.** First, **polar versus nonpolar** covalent bonds. When two atoms share electrons, sometimes the sharing is **unequal**: one atom pulls the shared electrons closer to itself. That makes its side **slightly negative** and the other side **slightly positive**. We call that a **polar** bond, and the star example is **water**: oxygen tugs the shared electrons toward itself. Picture a **lopsided tug-of-war** over the shared pair. (Important: these are only **slight, partial** charges, not full + or - ions like in an ionic bond.) When electrons are shared **equally**, the bond is **nonpolar**. Why care? Water's polarity makes it superb at **dissolving** things and gives it special behavior, something we will use later with solutions.

Next, **metallic bonding**. In a piece of metal, the atoms **pool their outer electrons** into a shared **'sea'** that flows freely among fixed **positive metal ions**. Those roaming electrons explain two everyday facts: metals **conduct** electricity and heat (the free electrons carry it along, like copper wire), and metals **bend** or can be hammered into shape without shattering (the ions slide past one another in the sea, like gold being shaped).

One last, lighter idea: separate from the bonds **inside** a molecule, there are **weaker attractions between whole molecules**. These between-molecule pulls make things stick, condense, and boil; they are why water droplets bead up and why ice is solid. Just keep the one idea: weaker attractions **between** molecules, not the strong bonds **within** them.

**Recap.** **Polar** covalent = **unequal** sharing (slight + and - sides, like water); **nonpolar** = equal sharing. **Metallic** = a **sea** of shared electrons around + ions, so metals **conduct** and **bend**. And weaker forces **between** molecules (distinct from bonds within them) help things stick and boil.

items.prompt: What is a POLAR covalent bond?
choices: A bond where electrons are shared exactly equally
A bond where one atom transfers electrons fully to the other
A covalent bond where electrons are shared UNEQUALLY, making one side slightly + and the other slightly -
A bond found only inside metals
items.explanation: A polar covalent bond is one where the shared electrons are pulled unequally, so one side is slightly negative and the other slightly positive. Equal sharing is NONPOLAR (choice 1); a full transfer is an ionic bond, not a polar covalent one (choice 2); and polar bonds are a kind of sharing, not a metallic thing (choice 4).

items.prompt: In a water molecule, why is the oxygen side slightly negative?
choices: Oxygen pulls the shared electrons closer to itself
Oxygen gives all its electrons to the hydrogens
Oxygen has no electrons near it at all
Oxygen turns into a full negative ion
items.explanation: Oxygen tugs the shared electrons closer, so its side gathers a slight negative charge, a lopsided tug-of-war. It does not give its electrons away (that would be transfer, not sharing) (choice 2); the shared electrons sit nearer to it, not absent (choice 3); and it gains only a slight, partial charge, not a full ion charge (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'In a polar bond, one atom has a FULL negative charge and the other a FULL positive charge.' What is the correct fix?
choices: The student is right; polar means full + and - charges
Polar bonds have only SLIGHT, partial charges, not full ones
Polar bonds have no charges anywhere
Polar bonds only happen between two metals
items.explanation: In a polar covalent bond the charges are only slight and partial, because the electrons are merely pulled closer, not handed over. Full + and - charges describe ions in an IONIC bond, so the student confuses partial with full (choice 1); polar bonds do have slight charges, so 'no charges' is wrong (choice 3); and they occur between nonmetals sharing, not two metals (choice 4).

items.prompt: When the two atoms in a covalent bond share the electrons EQUALLY, the bond is called:
choices: Polar
Ionic
Metallic
Nonpolar
items.explanation: Equal sharing gives a nonpolar bond. Polar is for UNEQUAL sharing (choice 1); ionic is a full transfer, not sharing at all (choice 2); and metallic is the electron-sea bond in metals (choice 3).

items.prompt: Why does water's polarity matter?
choices: It makes water great at dissolving things and gives it special behavior
It makes water unable to dissolve anything
It turns water into a metal
It has no effect on water at all
items.explanation: Water's slight + and - sides make it excellent at dissolving things and give it special behavior we will use with solutions later. Polarity helps dissolving rather than preventing it (choice 2); it does not make water a metal (choice 3); and it clearly does affect water (choice 4).

items.prompt: In METALLIC bonding, how are the outer electrons arranged?
choices: Each electron is locked tightly to one single atom
They are pooled into a shared 'sea' that flows among fixed positive metal ions
They are given away to a nearby nonmetal
They are shared as one pair between just two atoms
items.explanation: In a metal, the outer electrons pool into a shared sea that flows among fixed positive ions. They are not locked to single atoms (choice 1); no nonmetal receives them as in ionic bonding (choice 3); and it is a whole shared sea, not a single shared pair as in a covalent bond (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why do metals conduct electricity and heat so well?
choices: Because their atoms transfer electrons to each other one by one
Because their ions are perfectly still and never move
Because the free electrons in the shared sea can carry charge and heat along
Because metals have no electrons at all
items.explanation: The free, roaming electrons in the metallic sea carry electricity and heat through the metal, like in a copper wire. Metals do not bond by transferring electrons to each other (choice 1); it is the mobile electrons, not motionless ions, that conduct (choice 2); and metals are full of electrons (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why can a metal like gold be bent or hammered into shape without shattering?
choices: The positive ions can slide past one another within the electron sea
The metal first turns into a gas and back
Each atom gives an electron to the next, freezing them in place
The metal has ionic crystals that snap cleanly
items.explanation: Because the fixed positive ions can slide past one another while the electron sea keeps holding everything together, a metal bends rather than shatters. It does not vaporize to be shaped (choice 2); metallic bonding is pooling, not one-to-one transfer that locks atoms (choice 3); and brittle, snapping crystals describe ionic solids, which is why ionic compounds are brittle, unlike metals (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'Metals bond by each atom transferring electrons to its neighbor, like ionic bonding.' What is the correct fix?
choices: The student is right; metals bond by transferring to each other
Metals actually share single pairs like a covalent bond between two atoms
In metals, atoms POOL their outer electrons into a shared sea, rather than transferring them one-to-one
Metals do not bond at all
items.explanation: Metallic bonding is a shared sea of pooled electrons around positive ions, not a one-to-one transfer like ionic bonding, so the student's picture is wrong (choice 1). It is also not a single shared pair between just two atoms as in a covalent bond (choice 2), and metals certainly do bond, which is what holds them together (choice 4).

items.prompt: This lesson also mentions weaker attractions BETWEEN whole molecules. How do these differ from the bonds WITHIN a molecule?
choices: They are the same thing under a different name
They are weaker pulls between separate molecules, not the strong bonds holding one molecule together
They are stronger than any bond inside a molecule
They only exist inside metals
items.explanation: The forces between molecules are weaker pulls that act between separate whole molecules, distinct from the strong bonds that hold a single molecule together. They are not the same as bonds within a molecule (choice 1); they are weaker, not stronger, than those bonds (choice 3); and they are a general idea, not a metals-only one (choice 4).

items.prompt: Weaker attractions between whole molecules help explain which everyday observations?
choices: Why water droplets bead up and why ice is solid
Why metals conduct electricity
Why sodium gives an electron to chlorine
Why atoms have a nucleus at the center
items.explanation: These between-molecule pulls help things stick, condense, and boil, which is why water droplets bead up and ice holds together as a solid. Conducting electricity comes from the metallic electron sea (choice 2); the sodium-chlorine electron transfer is ionic bonding (choice 3); and the nucleus is about the atom's structure, not forces between molecules (choice 4).

lessons.title: Molecules and Compounds: Atoms Bonded Into a Unit
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Last module, atoms learned to stick together by sharing or transferring electrons. So what do you get once they are stuck? A **molecule**. Almost everything around you is made of these little joined-up groups of atoms.

**Build.** A **molecule** is **two or more atoms bonded together** into a single unit. A single water molecule, for example, is one oxygen atom bonded to two hydrogen atoms, all in one piece. Picture it as a tiny LEGO build: separate bricks (atoms) clicked firmly together into one object.

Now a closely related word. A **compound** is a substance made of **two or more DIFFERENT elements bonded together** (remember an **element** is just one kind of atom). Water is a compound because it is hydrogen *and* oxygen, two different elements, joined. So a molecule of water is also a unit of a compound.

Here is the key contrast, building on earlier modules. A **mixture** is **just stuff mixed together, not bonded** (salt stirred into water). Because nothing is joined, a mixture can be **separated again** by physical means, and each part stays itself. A compound is different: its atoms are truly **bonded**, so you cannot just stir them apart. Think of it this way: a **compound is like a word** whose specific letters are locked in order; a **mixture is like those letters tossed loose in a bag**.

**Recap.** A **molecule** is two or more atoms **bonded** into one unit; a **compound** is two or more **different** elements bonded together. A **mixture** is only mixed, not bonded, so it can be pulled apart again. Bonded word versus loose bag of letters.

items.prompt: What is a molecule?
choices: Two or more atoms bonded together into a single unit
A single atom sitting completely alone
Two atoms that are near each other but not bonded
Another name for a single electron
items.explanation: A molecule is two or more atoms bonded together as one unit. A lone atom by itself is not a molecule (choice 2); atoms merely near each other but not joined are a mixture, not a molecule (choice 3); and a molecule is built from atoms, not a single electron (choice 4).

items.prompt: What makes a substance a COMPOUND?
choices: It is made of only one kind of atom
It is two or more DIFFERENT elements bonded together
It is several things simply mixed but not bonded
It is a single atom with extra electrons
items.explanation: A compound is two or more different elements bonded together, like hydrogen and oxygen in water. One kind of atom is an element, not a compound (choice 1); things merely mixed and not bonded are a mixture (choice 3); and a single charged atom is an ion, not a compound (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why is water (hydrogen bonded to oxygen) called a compound?
choices: Because it is made of just one element
Because the hydrogen and oxygen are only mixed, not joined
Because it is two different elements bonded together
Because it can never be a molecule
items.explanation: Water is two different elements, hydrogen and oxygen, bonded together, so it is a compound. It is not a single element (choice 1); the atoms are truly bonded, not just mixed (choice 2); and a water unit is in fact a molecule as well as a compound (choice 4).

items.prompt: How is a MIXTURE different from a compound?
choices: In a mixture the parts are bonded; in a compound they are only mixed
In a mixture the parts are only mixed, not bonded, so it can be separated again
A mixture always contains exactly one element
A mixture and a compound are exactly the same thing
items.explanation: In a mixture the parts are just mixed, not bonded, so they keep their identity and can be separated again; in a compound the atoms are truly bonded. Choice 1 reverses this. A mixture has more than one kind of stuff, not one element (choice 3), and a mixture and compound are clearly different (choice 4).

items.prompt: Salt stirred into water can be separated again by letting the water dry off. This tells you salt water is:
choices: A compound, because its atoms are bonded
A single element
A mixture, because the parts are only mixed and not bonded
A molecule of one unit
items.explanation: Because it can be separated back into its parts, salt water is a mixture: nothing is bonded, just mixed. A compound's bonded atoms cannot be stirred apart like that (choice 1); it contains more than one kind of stuff, so not a single element (choice 2); and a loose mix is not one bonded molecule (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best fits a compound versus a mixture?
choices: A compound is like loose letters in a bag; a mixture is like a spelled-out word
A compound is like a word with letters locked in order; a mixture is like loose letters tossed in a bag
Both are exactly like a spelled-out word
Both are exactly like loose letters in a bag
items.explanation: A compound's atoms are bonded in a set arrangement, like a word with locked-in letters; a mixture is loose, like letters tossed in a bag. Choice 1 has the analogy backwards, and choices 3 and 4 ignore the difference between bonded and merely mixed.

items.prompt: An ELEMENT is best described as:
choices: Two or more different kinds of atom bonded together
One kind of atom
Any two substances stirred together
A substance that cannot be made of atoms
items.explanation: An element is just one kind of atom. Two or more different kinds bonded is a compound (choice 1); stirring substances together makes a mixture (choice 3); and elements are very much made of atoms (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'A compound is just different substances mixed together, so you can always stir them back apart.' What is the fix?
choices: The student is right; a compound is only a mixture
In a compound the atoms are BONDED, not just mixed, so you cannot simply stir them apart
A compound contains only one kind of atom
Compounds do not exist
items.explanation: The student has confused a compound with a mixture. In a compound the atoms are bonded together, so it cannot be separated by simple physical means the way a mixture can. A compound is not merely a mixture (choice 1); it has different elements, not one kind of atom (choice 3); and compounds certainly exist (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which statement best sums up this lesson?
choices: A molecule is atoms bonded into a unit; a compound is different elements bonded; a mixture is only mixed, not bonded
Molecules, compounds, and mixtures all mean exactly the same thing
A mixture is bonded, while a compound is just mixed
An element is two different kinds of atom bonded together
items.explanation: A molecule is two or more atoms bonded into one unit; a compound is two or more different elements bonded; a mixture is only mixed and can be separated again. The three are different (choice 2); it is the compound, not the mixture, whose parts are bonded (choice 3); and an element is one kind of atom (choice 4).

lessons.title: Reading Formulas and Counting Atoms
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You have seen 'H2O' on a water bottle. That little code is a **chemical formula**, and once you can read one, it tells you exactly which atoms make the unit and how many of each, like reading a recipe.

**Build.** A **chemical formula** uses **element symbols** (like H for hydrogen, O for oxygen) plus small **subscript** numbers. A **subscript** is the **small number written low**, right after a symbol, and it tells **how many of that atom** are in the unit. So **H2O** means **2 hydrogen + 1 oxygen**. Notice: oxygen has no number, and **no number means 1**. Likewise **CO2** is **1 carbon + 2 oxygen**, and **NaCl** (table salt) is **1 sodium + 1 chlorine**.

To **count the atoms** in a unit, read each subscript and add them. For water, H2O: 2 + 1 = **3 atoms** total. For carbon dioxide, CO2: 1 + 2 = **3 atoms** total. The subscript belongs only to the symbol right before it.

One forward-pointing note, kept light: the small number **below** (the subscript) counts **atoms inside one molecule**. A **big number in front** of a formula (called a coefficient) would instead mean **how many whole molecules** you have, but that is a topic for the reactions module. For now, just remember: **small-below = atoms in the unit**.

Finally, some elements never travel alone: they go as **pairs**, called **diatomic molecules** ('di' means two). **Oxygen is O2, nitrogen is N2, hydrogen is H2.** In fact the air you breathe is mostly **N2 and O2**. The atoms in O2 are the same element, oxygen, just buddied up.

**Recap.** A **formula** = symbols + subscripts telling how many of each atom (no number means 1); **count** by adding the subscripts (H2O = 3 atoms, CO2 = 3 atoms). Small number **below** counts atoms in the unit. Some elements go in pairs: **O2, N2, H2**.

items.prompt: In a chemical formula, what does a SUBSCRIPT (the small low number) tell you?
choices: How hot the substance is
How many of that atom are in the unit
The color of the substance
How heavy one atom is
items.explanation: A subscript tells how many of that atom are in the unit; in H2O the 2 means two hydrogen atoms. It says nothing about temperature (choice 1), color (choice 3), or the weight of a single atom (choice 4).

items.prompt: How many atoms in total are in one molecule of water, H2O?
choices: 2 atoms
4 atoms
1 atom
3 atoms
items.explanation: H2O has 2 hydrogen atoms plus 1 oxygen atom, and 2 + 1 = 3 atoms total. Counting only the hydrogens gives 2 (choice 1); 4 over-counts; and 1 ignores that there is more than one atom (choice 3).

items.prompt: In the formula CO2, how many oxygen atoms are there, and how many carbon atoms?
choices: 2 oxygen and 1 carbon
1 oxygen and 2 carbon
2 oxygen and 2 carbon
1 oxygen and 1 carbon
items.explanation: The subscript 2 sits after O, so there are 2 oxygen atoms, and C has no number, meaning 1 carbon. Choice 2 puts the subscript on the wrong symbol; choice 3 wrongly doubles the carbon; and choice 4 ignores the subscript on oxygen.

items.prompt: In the formula NaCl, why do we say there is 1 sodium and 1 chlorine?
choices: Because a missing number actually means zero
Because there is no subscript, and no number means 1
Because Na always means 2 atoms
Because Cl always means 3 atoms
items.explanation: When a symbol has no subscript, it means 1 of that atom, so NaCl is 1 sodium and 1 chlorine. A missing number means 1, not zero (choice 1), and neither Na nor Cl has a hidden 2 or 3 here (choices 3 and 4).

items.prompt: A diatomic molecule is:
choices: A molecule made of two atoms of the same element bonded together, like O2 or N2
A molecule made of two completely different elements
A single atom all by itself
Any molecule with three or more atoms
items.explanation: A diatomic molecule is two atoms of the same element bonded as a pair, like O2, N2, and H2 ('di' means two). It is not two different elements (choice 2); a lone atom is not diatomic (choice 3); and diatomic means two atoms, not three or more (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student looks at O2 and says, 'That's two different elements bonded together.' What is the fix?
choices: The student is right; O2 is two different elements
O2 is one element, oxygen, with two of its OWN atoms paired up
O2 is actually a single oxygen atom
O2 means oxygen plus carbon
items.explanation: Both atoms in O2 are oxygen, so it is one element whose atoms have paired up, not two different elements. The student is mistaken (choice 1); O2 is a pair, not a single atom (choice 3); and there is no carbon in O2 (choice 4).

items.prompt: The air you breathe is mostly made of which two diatomic molecules?
choices: H2O and CO2
NaCl and H2
N2 and O2
C and O
items.explanation: Air is mostly nitrogen and oxygen traveling as the diatomic pairs N2 and O2. Water and carbon dioxide are present only in small amounts (choice 1); salt and hydrogen gas are not the main components of air (choice 2); and lone C and O atoms are not how those elements travel in air (choice 4).

items.prompt: A subscript (the small number below) counts atoms inside ONE molecule. What would a big number written IN FRONT of a formula mean instead?
choices: How many whole molecules you have
How hot the molecule is
That the element has changed into a different one
Exactly the same thing as a subscript
items.explanation: A big number in front (a coefficient) counts whole molecules, while a subscript counts atoms within one molecule; full detail comes in the reactions module. It is not about temperature (choice 2), it does not change the element (choice 3), and it is not the same as a subscript (choice 4).

items.prompt: A friend writes H2O and says the little 2 changes the H into a brand-new element. What is the correct fix?
choices: The friend is right; a subscript turns H into something else
The subscript only tells HOW MANY hydrogen atoms there are; it is still hydrogen
The subscript turns hydrogen into oxygen
The subscript removes all the hydrogen
items.explanation: A subscript only counts atoms; the 2 in H2O means two hydrogen atoms, and they are still hydrogen. It does not change the element (choice 1), does not turn hydrogen into oxygen (choice 3), and does not remove the hydrogen (choice 4).

items.prompt: Count the total atoms in CO2.
choices: 2 atoms
3 atoms
1 atom
4 atoms
items.explanation: CO2 has 1 carbon and 2 oxygen, and 1 + 2 = 3 atoms total. Counting only the oxygens gives 2 (choice 1); 1 ignores the oxygen subscript (choice 3); and 4 over-counts (choice 4).

lessons.title: Naming Basics and Models of Molecules
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Why is 'NaCl' also just called *table salt*, and 'CO2' called *carbon dioxide*? Names follow a few readable patterns. You do not need to memorize tables, just enough to recognize what a name is telling you.

**Build.** Start with **simple ionic compounds** (a **metal** bonded to a **nonmetal**, from the bonding module). The pattern: **name the metal, then the nonmetal with its ending changed to '-ide'.** So sodium + chlorine becomes **sodium chloride** (that is NaCl, table salt); magnesium + oxygen becomes **magnesium oxide**. The metal keeps its name; the nonmetal gets the '-ide' tail.

Now **simple molecules** made of two nonmetals often use small **prefixes** that tell you how many atoms. The two to know: **'mono' means 1** and **'di' means 2.** So **carbon dioxide** is **1 carbon, 2 oxygen** (CO2) and **carbon monoxide** is **1 carbon, 1 oxygen** (CO). Mixing up mono and di is a classic slip, so read carefully: 'di' = 2, 'mono' = 1.

Many everyday substances also have plain **common names**: **water** is H2O, **table salt** is sodium chloride, and **baking soda** is another common name. The common name and the chemical name point to the same stuff.

Finally, chemists draw molecules as **models** to picture their **shape**. In a **ball-and-stick model**, **balls are atoms** and **sticks are bonds**; a **space-filling model** shows the atoms as packed-together blobs. Water, for instance, is **bent**, not in a straight line. Shape is worth a quick note because, as a light forward pointer, a molecule's shape affects how it behaves. Think of a **tinker-toy** model: balls joined by sticks showing the real arrangement.

**Recap.** Simple ionic names: **metal + nonmetal-'ide'** (sodium chloride). Simple molecule prefixes: **'mono' = 1, 'di' = 2** (carbon monoxide vs carbon dioxide). Everyday things have **common names** (water, table salt). **Models** (ball-and-stick, space-filling) show a molecule's **shape**.

items.prompt: For a simple ionic compound of a metal and a nonmetal, how is it usually named?
choices: Name the nonmetal first, then the metal unchanged
Name the metal, then the nonmetal with an '-ide' ending
Name only the metal and ignore the nonmetal
Give it a number instead of a name
items.explanation: The pattern is to name the metal, then the nonmetal with its ending changed to '-ide', as in sodium chloride. The metal comes first, not the nonmetal (choice 1); both parts are named (choice 3); and it gets a name, not just a number (choice 4).

items.prompt: Sodium bonds with chlorine to form NaCl. What is its name?
choices: Chlorine sodide
Sodium chlorine
Sodium chloride
Sodium dichloride
items.explanation: Name the metal (sodium), then the nonmetal with an '-ide' ending (chloride), giving sodium chloride. The metal goes first, not the nonmetal (choice 1); the nonmetal's ending changes to '-ide', so 'chlorine' is wrong (choice 2); and there is only one chlorine, so 'di' does not belong (choice 4).

items.prompt: In naming simple molecules, the prefix 'di' means:
items.explanation: 'Di' means 2, as in carbon dioxide (two oxygen atoms). 'Mono' is the prefix for 1 (choice 1), and 'di' does not mean 3 or 10 (choices 3 and 4).

items.prompt: Carbon dioxide is CO2 and carbon monoxide is CO. What is the difference in their names telling you?
choices: 'Di' means 2 oxygen atoms; 'mono' means 1 oxygen atom
'Di' means 1 oxygen atom; 'mono' means 2 oxygen atoms
Both names mean exactly the same number of oxygen atoms
The names tell you the color, not the number of atoms
items.explanation: 'Di' = 2, so carbon dioxide has 2 oxygen; 'mono' = 1, so carbon monoxide has 1 oxygen. Choice 2 swaps the prefixes, a common slip; the two are not the same (choice 3); and prefixes count atoms, not color (choice 4).

items.prompt: Magnesium bonds with oxygen. Using the metal + nonmetal-'ide' pattern, the name is:
choices: Oxygen magneside
Magnesium oxide
Magnesium oxygen
Magnesium dioxide
items.explanation: Name the metal (magnesium), then the nonmetal with an '-ide' ending (oxide), giving magnesium oxide. The metal comes first (choice 1); the ending becomes '-ide', so 'oxygen' is wrong (choice 3); and the plain pattern here does not add 'di' (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday common name matches the substance H2O?
choices: Table salt
Baking soda
Water
Carbon dioxide
items.explanation: H2O is the compound we call water. Table salt is sodium chloride (choice 1), baking soda is a different substance (choice 2), and carbon dioxide is CO2 (choice 4).

items.prompt: In a ball-and-stick model of a molecule, what do the balls and sticks represent?
choices: Balls are atoms and sticks are the bonds between them
Balls are bonds and sticks are atoms
Balls are whole molecules and sticks are mixtures
Balls are electrons and sticks are protons
items.explanation: In a ball-and-stick model, each ball is an atom and each stick is a bond joining two atoms. Choice 2 reverses this; balls are atoms, not whole molecules or mixtures (choice 3); and they are atoms and bonds, not electrons and protons (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why do chemists bother drawing models of molecules?
choices: To show the molecule's shape, which affects how it behaves
To change the molecule into a different element
Because models weigh exactly the same as the molecule
To prove molecules have no shape at all
items.explanation: Models picture a molecule's shape, and shape affects how the molecule behaves. Drawing a model does not change the element (choice 2), it is a picture rather than a weight measurement (choice 3), and molecules do have shape, such as water being bent (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says carbon MONoxide must have 2 oxygen atoms because 'mono sounds like more.' What is the fix?
choices: The student is right; 'mono' means 2
'Mono' means 1, so carbon monoxide has just 1 oxygen atom
'Mono' means 3 oxygen atoms
'Mono' tells you the color, not the number
items.explanation: 'Mono' means 1, so carbon monoxide (CO) has exactly 1 oxygen atom; it is 'di' that means 2. The student has the prefix wrong (choice 1), 'mono' is not 3 (choice 3), and prefixes count atoms, not color (choice 4).

lessons.title: The Counting Problem and the Chemist's Dozen
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You already know a formula like H2O tells you how many atoms are in a unit. But here is a problem: atoms are unbelievably tiny, and even a small pinch of any substance holds a number of atoms so huge you could never count them one by one. And you cannot weigh a single atom on a kitchen scale. So how does a chemist ever know how many atoms they have?

**Build.** Think about how you handle other 'too many to count' things. You do not count individual grains of rice, you **weigh** them. You do not count sheets of paper one by one, you buy a **ream** (a fixed number). And you count eggs by the **dozen**. A 'dozen' is just a **counting word** that always means a fixed number: **12**. Say 'a dozen' and everyone knows you mean 12, whether it is eggs or pencils.

Chemists borrowed this trick. They invented a counting word for atoms called the **mole**. A **mole** is simply a fixed, gigantic number of particles: **about 6 followed by 23 zeros** (written as about 6 x 10 to the 23, and called **Avogadro's number**). That is roughly 600 billion trillion. So a mole is to a chemist what a dozen is to a baker, only the pile is enormous because atoms are so small.

Why such a strange, huge number? Because atoms are so tiny that you need a giant group before you have a weighable, workable amount. One mole of a substance is a pinch you can actually see and hold, yet it contains about 6 x 10 to the 23 atoms.

**Recap.** Atoms are too tiny and too many to count one by one, so chemists count them in huge groups. The **mole** is a counting word, like a dozen, that always means a fixed huge number of particles: **about 6 x 10 to the 23** (Avogadro's number). Dozen = 12; mole = about 6 x 10 to the 23.

items.prompt: Why can't a chemist just count the atoms in a pinch of a substance one by one?
choices: Atoms are far too tiny and far too many to count individually
Atoms keep changing into different elements while you count
There are only about a dozen atoms, so counting is pointless
Atoms are too large to fit in a container
items.explanation: Atoms are unbelievably tiny and even a pinch holds a number too huge to count one by one, which is the whole reason chemists count in big groups. Atoms do not change element on their own here (choice 2); a pinch holds a giant number, not a dozen (choice 3); and atoms are extremely small, not large (choice 4).

items.prompt: A 'dozen' is used as an example of what kind of word?
choices: A word for how heavy one egg is
A counting word that always stands for a fixed number (12)
A word that means a different number each time you use it
A word for the color of something
items.explanation: A dozen is a counting word that always means a fixed number, 12, no matter what you are counting. It does not describe weight (choice 1); it is fixed, not changing (choice 3); and it has nothing to do with color (choice 4).

items.prompt: What is a MOLE in chemistry?
choices: A small number, around ten or so particles
Another word for a single atom
A fixed, gigantic number of particles, about 6 x 10 to the 23
A unit of temperature
items.explanation: A mole is a counting word for a fixed, huge number of particles, about 6 x 10 to the 23 (Avogadro's number). It is enormous, not small like ten (choice 1) - thinking the mole is a small number is a common slip; it is a giant group, not one atom (choice 2); and it counts particles, not temperature (choice 4).

items.prompt: About how many particles are in one mole?
choices: About 12
About 100
About 1,000
About 6 followed by 23 zeros (6 x 10 to the 23)
items.explanation: One mole is about 6 followed by 23 zeros, written 6 x 10 to the 23 and called Avogadro's number. 12 is a dozen, not a mole (choice 1); a hundred or a thousand are still far too small (choices 2 and 3). The whole point is that the mole is gigantic.

items.prompt: The mole is compared to a dozen because both are:
choices: Counting words that each stand for a fixed number of things
Exactly the same size
Words that mean 'very heavy'
Ways to measure temperature
items.explanation: Both a dozen and a mole are counting words: each always stands for a fixed number of items. They are not the same size - a dozen is 12, a mole is about 6 x 10 to the 23 (choice 2); neither means heavy (choice 3); and neither measures temperature (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why did chemists pick such a huge number for the mole?
choices: To make chemistry as confusing as possible
Because atoms are so tiny that you need a giant group before you have a workable, weighable amount
Because there are only ever a few atoms in anything
Because bigger numbers are always more accurate
items.explanation: Atoms are so small that you need an enormous group before the amount is big enough to see, hold, and weigh, so the mole is huge. It was chosen for convenience, not to confuse (choice 1); substances contain vast numbers of atoms, not a few (choice 3); and the size is about practicality, not accuracy (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday habit is most like using the mole to handle atoms?
choices: Painting each grain of rice a different color
Weighing rice or buying paper by the ream instead of counting each piece
Refusing to use rice because there is too much
Counting every single grain of rice by hand
items.explanation: Just as we weigh rice or buy paper by the ream rather than counting each piece, chemists handle atoms in huge fixed groups (moles). Painting grains is unrelated (choice 1); chemists do use the substance (choice 3); and the whole idea is to avoid counting each piece by hand (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'A mole must be a small amount, like a handful of atoms.' What is the fix?
choices: The student is right; a mole is just a few atoms
A mole is actually a fixed, gigantic number of particles, about 6 x 10 to the 23
A mole means exactly 12 atoms, like a dozen
A mole changes its size depending on the substance
items.explanation: A mole is not small at all; it is a fixed, gigantic count of about 6 x 10 to the 23 particles. So the student is mistaken (choice 1); a mole is far larger than a dozen's 12 (choice 3); and the count is always the same fixed number, no matter the substance (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which statement best sums up this lesson?
choices: Atoms are easy to count one by one with a kitchen scale
A mole is a tiny number used only for large objects
Atoms are too tiny and too many to count, so chemists use the mole, a counting word for about 6 x 10 to the 23 particles
A dozen and a mole are the same size
items.explanation: Because atoms are too tiny and numerous to count, chemists count them in huge groups using the mole, about 6 x 10 to the 23 particles. You cannot count atoms with a kitchen scale (choice 1); the mole is gigantic, not tiny (choice 2); and a dozen (12) is nothing like a mole (choice 4).

lessons.title: Molar Mass: Weighing Out a Mole
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A mole is a count, but you cannot count to 6 x 10 to the 23. So how do you actually get one mole into a beaker? Here is the genius part: you **weigh it**. And the periodic table tells you exactly how much it should weigh.

**Build.** Recall that every cell on the periodic table shows an **atomic mass** number (carbon's cell shows about 12). Here is the magic rule: **that number is the mass, in grams, of one mole of that element.** This is called the **molar mass** - the mass of one mole, measured in **grams per mole** (g/mol).

So carbon's atomic mass is about 12, which means **1 mole of carbon weighs about 12 grams**. For other common elements (using clean rounded masses): hydrogen is **1 g/mol**, carbon is **12 g/mol**, nitrogen is **14 g/mol**, and oxygen is **16 g/mol**. Weigh out 16 grams of oxygen atoms and you have exactly one mole of them.

What about a **compound**, made of several atoms bonded together? Easy: **add up the masses of all the atoms in its formula.** Take water, **H2O**: that is 2 hydrogen (1 each) plus 1 oxygen (16), so 1 + 1 + 16 = **18 g/mol**. One mole of water weighs 18 grams. Take carbon dioxide, **CO2**: 1 carbon (12) plus 2 oxygen (16 each), so 12 + 16 + 16 = **44 g/mol**. One mole of carbon dioxide weighs 44 grams. You are just reading masses off the table (the 'menu') and adding.

**Recap.** **Molar mass** is the mass of one mole, in **grams per mole**, and you read it straight off the periodic table: the atomic mass number IS the grams per mole. For a compound, **add the masses of every atom**: water H2O = 1 + 1 + 16 = **18 g/mol**; carbon dioxide CO2 = 12 + 16 + 16 = **44 g/mol**.

items.prompt: Where do you find the molar mass of a single element?
choices: You have to measure it with a stopwatch
It is the atomic mass number shown on the periodic table
Molar mass has nothing to do with the periodic table
You guess it from the element's color
items.explanation: The atomic mass number on the periodic table IS the molar mass: the grams in one mole of that element. It is not timed with a stopwatch (choice 1); thinking molar mass is unrelated to the table is a common misconception (choice 3); and color does not tell you mass (choice 4).

items.prompt: Carbon's atomic mass is about 12. What does one mole of carbon weigh?
choices: About 12 grams
About 1 gram
About 6 grams
About 24 grams
items.explanation: The atomic mass (12) is the grams per mole, so one mole of carbon weighs about 12 grams. 1 gram would be one mole of hydrogen (choice 2); 6 is the atomic number, not the mass (choice 3); and 24 grams would be two moles of carbon, not one (choice 4).

items.prompt: What does 'molar mass' mean?
choices: The number of atoms in a single molecule
The temperature at which a mole melts
The mass of one mole of a substance, in grams per mole
The mass of a single atom on a kitchen scale
items.explanation: Molar mass is the mass of one mole of a substance, measured in grams per mole. It is a mass, not a count of atoms (choice 1); it is not a melting temperature (choice 2); and it is the mass of a whole mole, not of one tiny atom (choice 4).

items.prompt: Using rounded masses H = 1 and O = 16, what is the molar mass of water, H2O?
choices: 16 g/mol
17 g/mol
34 g/mol
18 g/mol
items.explanation: Water is 2 hydrogen (1 each) plus 1 oxygen (16): 1 + 1 + 16 = 18 g/mol. Choice 1 forgets the hydrogens; choice 2 counts only one hydrogen; and choice 3 wrongly doubles the oxygen. The correct clean sum is 18.

items.prompt: Using rounded masses C = 12 and O = 16, what is the molar mass of carbon dioxide, CO2?
choices: 28 g/mol
44 g/mol
32 g/mol
40 g/mol
items.explanation: CO2 is 1 carbon (12) plus 2 oxygen (16 each): 12 + 16 + 16 = 44 g/mol. Choice 1 uses only one oxygen (12 + 16); choice 3 forgets the carbon (16 + 16); and choice 4 is just a wrong sum. The correct total is 44.

items.prompt: How do you find the molar mass of a compound from its formula?
choices: Add up the masses of all the atoms in the formula
Use only the mass of the very first atom
Subtract the smaller atomic mass from the larger one
Count the atoms but ignore their masses
items.explanation: You add the masses of every atom in the formula, reading each mass off the periodic table. Using only the first atom ignores the rest (choice 2); molar mass is a sum, not a subtraction (choice 3); and you must use the masses, not just the count (choice 4).

items.prompt: Using rounded masses, the molar mass of oxygen gas, O2 (two oxygen atoms, 16 each), is:
choices: 16 g/mol
8 g/mol
32 g/mol
18 g/mol
items.explanation: O2 is two oxygen atoms at 16 each: 16 + 16 = 32 g/mol. 16 counts only one oxygen (choice 1); 8 wrongly halves it (choice 2); and 18 is water's molar mass, not O2 (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'Molar mass has nothing to do with the periodic table; you just memorize it.' What is the fix?
choices: The student is right; the table is no help here
The atomic mass number ON the periodic table IS the molar mass, in grams per mole
Molar mass is found only by tasting the substance
Molar mass is the same number for every element
items.explanation: The table is exactly where molar mass comes from: the atomic mass number is the grams per mole. So the student is mistaken (choice 1); you never taste chemicals to find mass (choice 3); and each element has its own molar mass, not one shared number (choice 4).

items.prompt: A friend calculates water's molar mass as 1 + 1 + 16 = 19. What went wrong?
choices: Nothing; 19 is correct
The oxygen should have been counted twice
The sum is just added up wrong; 1 + 1 + 16 = 18, not 19
Hydrogen's mass should be 16
items.explanation: The atoms and masses are right (2 hydrogen at 1, 1 oxygen at 16), but the addition slipped: 1 + 1 + 16 = 18, not 19. There is only one oxygen in H2O (choice 2); and hydrogen's rounded mass is 1, not 16 (choice 4). Careful adding gives 18.

items.prompt: If the periodic table lists oxygen's atomic mass as about 16, then one mole of oxygen atoms weighs:
choices: About 16 grams
About 1 gram
About 32 grams
About 8 grams
items.explanation: The atomic mass (16) is the grams in one mole, so one mole of oxygen atoms weighs about 16 grams. 1 gram is hydrogen's molar mass (choice 2); 32 grams would be two moles of oxygen atoms (choice 3); and 8 grams is half a mole (choice 4).

lessons.title: Converting Grams, Moles and Particles - and Why It Matters
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Now the payoff. Molar mass is a **bridge**: it lets you cross between **grams** (which you can weigh) and **moles** or **particles** (which you can count). And the math stays gentle - just easy multiples, no calculator needed.

**Build.** The reasoning is plain proportional thinking. You know **1 mole of water weighs 18 grams**. So:

- **36 grams** of water is **2 moles** (because 18 + 18 = 36, that is two lots of 18).
- **9 grams** of water is **half a mole** (because half of 18 is 9).

The same idea works for any substance. **1 mole of carbon is 12 grams**, so **24 grams of carbon is 2 moles** (because 12 + 12 = 24). To go from grams to moles, ask: 'How many molar-masses fit into my grams?'

And moles connect to the **particle count** too. Since **1 mole is about 6 x 10 to the 23 particles**, then **2 moles is about 12 x 10 to the 23 particles**, and **half a mole is about 3 x 10 to the 23 particles**. Moles sit in the middle: grams on one side, number of particles on the other.

**Why does any of this matter?** Because a chemical **reaction combines atoms in fixed whole-number ratios** (you will balance these next module). To make a reaction work, a chemist needs the **right number of atoms** of each kind - but they cannot count atoms. Instead they **weigh out the right number of moles**, using molar mass as the bridge. It is like a recipe: you need the right ratio of eggs to flour, and the mole is how you 'count' atoms by weighing them.

**Recap.** Molar mass bridges **grams (weigh) and moles/particles (count)**. With water at 18 g/mol: 36 g = 2 mol, 9 g = half a mole; with carbon at 12 g/mol: 24 g = 2 mol. And 1 mole = about 6 x 10 to the 23 particles, 2 moles = about 12 x 10 to the 23. This lets chemists weigh out the right number of atoms for a reaction.

items.prompt: One mole of water weighs 18 grams. How many moles are in 36 grams of water?
choices: Half a mole
2 moles
18 moles
36 moles
items.explanation: Since 1 mole is 18 g, and 18 + 18 = 36, then 36 g is 2 moles. Half a mole would be 9 g (choice 1); 18 and 36 are gram amounts, not the number of moles (choices 3 and 4).

items.prompt: One mole of water weighs 18 grams. How much does HALF a mole of water weigh?
choices: 36 grams
18 grams
9 grams
2 grams
items.explanation: Half a mole is half the mass: half of 18 g is 9 g. 36 g would be 2 moles (choice 1); 18 g is a full mole (choice 2); and 2 g does not come from halving 18 (choice 4).

items.prompt: One mole of carbon weighs 12 grams. How many moles are in 24 grams of carbon?
choices: 2 moles
Half a mole
12 moles
24 moles
items.explanation: Since 1 mole of carbon is 12 g, and 12 + 12 = 24, then 24 g is 2 moles. Half a mole would be 6 g (choice 2); 12 and 24 are gram amounts, not mole counts (choices 3 and 4).

items.prompt: If 1 mole is about 6 x 10 to the 23 particles, how many particles are in 2 moles?
choices: About 3 x 10 to the 23
Still about 6 x 10 to the 23
About 12 x 10 to the 23
Exactly 2 particles
items.explanation: Two moles is twice the particles: about 6 x 10 to the 23 doubled is about 12 x 10 to the 23. About 3 x 10 to the 23 would be half a mole (choice 1); 6 x 10 to the 23 is just one mole (choice 2); and 2 moles is a huge number of particles, not 2 (choice 4).

items.prompt: What job does molar mass do when converting amounts?
choices: It is a bridge between grams (weighable) and moles or particles (countable)
It changes the substance into a different element
It tells you the color of the substance
It has no use once you know the formula
items.explanation: Molar mass bridges grams and moles/particles: weigh the grams, then use molar mass to find the moles. It does not change the element (choice 2) or reveal color (choice 3), and it is essential, not useless (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why do chemists need the mole at all?
choices: To make small numbers look bigger for fun
So they can count atoms by weighing, getting the right number of atoms for a reaction
Because atoms can easily be counted one at a time anyway
To measure how hot a reaction gets
items.explanation: Reactions need the right number of atoms, and since atoms cannot be counted directly, the mole lets chemists count them by weighing. It is not for show (choice 1); atoms cannot be counted one at a time (choice 3); and the mole is about amount, not temperature (choice 4).

items.prompt: A reaction combines atoms in fixed whole-number ratios, like a recipe. How does the mole help with this?
choices: It lets a chemist weigh out the right number of atoms, since they can't be counted
It removes the need for any particular ratio
It makes the atoms change their ratio randomly
It tells the chemist the price of each atom
items.explanation: Like measuring the right ratio of eggs to flour, the mole lets a chemist weigh out the right number of atoms for the reaction's ratio. The ratio still matters (choice 2); the mole does not randomize ratios (choice 3); and it is about amount, not price (choice 4).

items.prompt: One mole of water is 18 g. How much does 10 moles of water weigh?
choices: 18 grams
28 grams
180 grams
1.8 grams
items.explanation: Ten moles is ten times the mass: 18 g times 10 is 180 g. 18 g is just one mole (choice 1); 28 g comes from adding 10 instead of multiplying (choice 2); and 1.8 g divides instead of multiplies (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'Grams and moles are just two words for the same thing.' What is the fix?
choices: The student is right; grams and moles are identical
Grams measure WEIGHT, while moles count the NUMBER of particles; molar mass links the two
Grams count particles and moles measure weight, so they are swapped
Moles are a kind of gram used only for gases
items.explanation: Grams are a weight; moles are a count of particles. They are different ideas, joined by molar mass. So they are not identical (choice 1); it is grams that weigh and moles that count, not the reverse (choice 3); and a mole is not a kind of gram (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which statement best ties this module together?
choices: Atoms are easy to count, so the mole is unnecessary
Molar mass is unrelated to the periodic table
A mole is a small number of atoms you can count by hand
Atoms are too many to count, so chemists use the mole (about 6 x 10 to the 23); the table's mass = grams per mole, and molar mass bridges grams and particles
items.explanation: The mole (about 6 x 10 to the 23) lets chemists count atoms by weighing: the table's atomic mass is grams per mole, and molar mass bridges grams and particle count. Atoms cannot be counted by hand (choices 1 and 3), and molar mass comes straight off the periodic table (choice 2).
